message.content:
Implement the following plan: # Plan: 50-State ICE Activity Tracker for justNICE ## Context justNICE already has two ICE activity blog posts (ATX and MN). The user wants to: 1. Expand Austin → full Texas statewide coverage (hub + regional pages) 2. Create skeleton pages for all 50 states 3. Build an n8n automation pipeline for daily updates 4. Compile a comprehensive resources sidebar linking 19+ tracking tools/sources ## Architecture ### URL Structure ``` /blog/ice-activity/ ← US hub/index page (links to all 50 states) /blog/ice-activity/texas.html ← Texas hub (links to regional pages) /blog/ice-activity/texas/austin.html ← Austin regional (existing ATX post, moved) /blog/ice-activity/texas/dfw.html ← Dallas-Fort Worth /blog/ice-activity/texas/houston.html ← Houston / Harris County /blog/ice-activity/texas/san-antonio.html /blog/ice-activity/texas/rio-grande-valley.html /blog/ice-activity/texas/el-paso.html /blog/ice-activity/texas/other.html ← Lubbock, Corpus, Tyler, Amarillo, etc. /blog/ice-activity/minnesota.html ← MN (existing, moved) /blog/ice-activity/california.html ← Skeleton /blog/ice-activity/florida.html ← Skeleton ... (all 50 states) ``` ### What gets built TODAY (this session) #### Phase 1: Texas statewide page (`texas.html`) - **Hub page** with statewide context (SB 8, Abbott's actions, DPS 287(g), enforcement stats, school/church impacts, construction industry impact, deportation flights, legal challenges) - Links to regional sub-pages - "Last updated" timestamp and reverse-chronological entry format - Resources sidebar with all 19+ tracking tools #### Phase 2: Texas regional pages (6 pages) Using research from the TX agent, create pages for: 1. **Austin** — move existing ATX content, add Feb-Mar 2026 updates 2. **DFW** — 84 arrests/day, probation office arrests, 100 arrests/day field office 3. **Houston** — 646 arrests Feb-Mar, Harris County jail detainers, HPD policy changes 4. **San Antonio** — 7-hour city council meeting, $66M warehouse purchase, vehicle ramming, home raid 5. **Rio Grande Valley** — Mariachi family detention, worksite raids, LUPE signs, Dilley 6. **El Paso** — Camp East Montana (homicide ruling, measles, possible closure), construction raids, National Shutdown #### Phase 3: US hub + 49 state pages (seeded with data) - **US index page** with list linking to all 50 states, organized by region - **Every state page** gets researched and seeded with: - State's ICE field office(s) and AOR (area of responsibility) - 287(g) agreements count and key participating counties - State-level sanctuary/anti-sanctuary laws - Known incidents from Jan-Mar 2026 (web search per state) - Key local news sources for that state - Governor/AG stance on ICE cooperation - This requires ~50 web searches (one per state cluster) — will batch via agents - Pages with no known 2026 activity still get the policy context + "Submit a report" callout #### Phase 4: Resources sidebar (shared across all pages) A standardized sidebar/section on every page linking to: **Structured Data Sources:** - Deportation Data Project (deportationdata.org) — Excel/ZIP downloads - TRAC Immigration (tracreports.org) — queryable court data - CourtListener (courtlistener.com) — federal court API - FlightAware ICE fleet (fleet code RPN) — live deportation flights **Tracking Maps & Tools:** - DetentionReports.com — facility-level detention data - ICE List (icelist.is) — wiki of incidents, agents, facilities - ResistMap (resistmap.com) — crowdsourced ICE sighting map - DeFlock.me — ALPR camera map - HaveIBeenFlocked.com — plate reader lookup - ICE in My Area (iceinmyarea.org) — SMS alerts - Immigrant Defense Project ICEwatch — raid tactics map - Deportation Tracker Live (deportationtracker.live) — real-time tracker **Journalism:** - Drop Site News (dropsitenews.com) — Substack, RSS available - ProPublica Immigration (propublica.org/topics/immigration) - TRAC Reports (tracreports.org) - Witness at the Border → Human Rights First ICE Flight Monitor **Legal & Policy:** - Immigration Policy Tracking Project (immpolicytracking.org) — Stanford/Yale - National Immigration Litigation Alliance — Litigation Navigator - ACLU ICE Tracking — FOIA documents - American Immigration Council — Map the Impact tool #### Phase 5: n8n automation workflow Design an n8n workflow that: 1. **Monitors RSS feeds** — Drop Site News, ProPublica, state-specific news sources 2. **Checks structured APIs** — CourtListener (for new immigration rulings), TRAC (for new data releases) 3. **Scrapes key pages** — DetentionReports.com (biweekly ICE data), ICE.gov press releases 4. **Generates daily digest** — summarized by state, with source URLs 5. **Creates draft entries** — formatted HTML snippets ready to paste into state pages 6. **Sends notification** — Slack/email to human for review before publishing n8n workflow nodes: - Schedule Trigger (daily at 6am CT) - HTTP Request nodes for RSS feeds (10+ feeds) - HTML Extract nodes for scraping - AI Agent node (Claude) for summarization and state classification - Code node for HTML formatting - Webhook/notification for review queue ### Template Structure Each state page follows the same HTML template based on the existing ATX/MN pattern: - justNICE header/nav (copy from existing) - Breadcrumb: Home / Blog / ICE Activity / [State] - Article header with state name, last-updated date - "How to submit a report" callout box - Content area: entries in reverse chronological order (newest first) - Each entry: `

` with date + location, `

` with sourced narrative, footnote refs - Resources sidebar - Footnotes section - justNICE footer ### Key Files to Modify - `/blog.html` — add ICE Activity Tracker section/card linking to hub - Existing ATX post stays at current URL (don't break links) but also linked from TX hub - Existing MN post stays at current URL but also linked from US hub ### What I will NOT do - Move/rename existing ATX or MN files (would break existing links) - Generate hero images (placeholder or no-image layout) - Build the full n8n workflow (will design the spec, user needs to create in n8n UI or via API) - Research X.com accounts (deferred to follow-up session) ### Session scope reality check Building 50+ seeded state pages is a LOT of content. Realistic plan for this session: 1. **Template + Texas hub + 6 TX regionals** — full content, today 2. **US hub page** — today 3. **State research** — batch via parallel agents (5-10 states per agent) 4. **State page generation** — may span into next session if context runs low 5. **n8n workflow spec** — document, not implementation 6. **SFTP deploy** — whatever is ready ## Execution Order 1. Create directory structure: `/blog/ice-activity/` and `/blog/ice-activity/texas/` 2. Create the shared HTML template (header, nav, sidebar, footer as reusable blocks) 3. Build Texas hub page with statewide content 4. Build 6 Texas regional pages with researched content 5. Build US hub/index page 6. Create 49 state skeleton pages (batch — template + state-specific data) 7. Update `blog.html` to feature the tracker 8. Write n8n workflow spec document 9. Deploy via SFTP to commoncloud.cc ## Verification - Open each page locally to check rendering - Verify all internal links work - Check footnote anchors - Test mobile responsiveness (Tailwind is responsive by default) - SFTP upload and verify on https://justnice.us/blog/ice-activity/ If you need specific details from before exiting plan mode (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/kristinesocall/.claude/projects/-Users-kristinesocall-Library-CloudStorage-ProtonDrive-gifteddreamers-proton-me-folder-2025-12-18-CC-kristinesocall/15c9c139-43b5-4e2d-ac43-0a6e8b067898.jsonl

294→ ICE Activity Within a 2-Hour Radius of Austin, TX 295→

296→

297→ The past 30 days have seen a significant escalation of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations in and around Austin, Texas, accompanied by community protests, policy changes, and legal developments. Below is a comprehensive timeline of reported incidents from January 10 to February 10, 2026, organized by location and date, with all sources cited. 298→

299→
300→ 307→ 308→ 309→ 310→
311→ 312→
313→ 314→ 315→

Austin / Travis County

316→ 317→

January 5 — Deportation After 911 Call

318→ 319→

320→ Austin Police responded to a disturbance call on Blue Stem Trail. The woman who called, Karen Gutiérrez-Castellanos, a Honduran national, was found to have an ICE administrative warrant in the database. APD notified ICE, which took custody of Gutiérrez-Castellanos and her 5-year-old daughter — a U.S. citizen. Both were deported to Honduras within days, without an opportunity to speak with an attorney or appear before a judge. The incident became a catalyst for major policy changes at APD and citywide outrage.[1][2][3] 321→

322→ 323→

January 12–13 — ICE Chase on SH 130 and Confusion at Travis County Sheriff’s Office

324→ 325→

326→ Federal and state officers pursuing suspects on SH 130 south of Austin called for assistance from the Travis County Sheriff’s Office. Due to confusion about the nature of the request and communications involving Pflugerville PD, a TCSO supervisor initially declined to assist. The chase ended on Pearce Lane near the Sun Chase subdivision, northeast of Circuit of the Americas. A separate, uninvolved motorist was involved in a crash. Sheriff Sally Hernandez issued a statement saying TCSO’s policy is not to decline assistance to other law enforcement agencies and that “recent events on the national stage” may have affected the response.[4] 327→

328→ 329→

January 13 — Incident Near Hutto (Williamson County)

330→ 331→

332→ On the same day, Williamson County deputies responded around 5 a.m. to reports of gunshots fired from a car on Highway 79 near Hutto. Four occupants, identified as undocumented migrants, were taken into custody. One was jailed on charges related to the shooting, and the other three were turned over to ICE.[4] 333→

334→ 335→

January 14 — APD Announces Policy Change on ICE Cooperation

336→ 337→

338→ APD Chief Lisa Davis announced the department would update its “general orders” to explicitly allow officers to cooperate more fully with federal immigration officers in executing immigration warrants. Under the new policy, officers who decide to report someone to ICE must first notify a duty commander. Davis stated, “Senate Bill 4 does not allow us to tell officers they cannot call ICE on these things.” Multiple Austin City Council members said they do not anticipate this leading to more cooperation in practice.[5][1] 339→

340→ 341→

January 18 — ICE Activity on SH 195, Williamson County

342→ 343→

344→ ICE activity was spotted along State Highway 195 in Williamson County, north of Austin. A KXAN reporter described witnessing a traffic stop in which a person was handcuffed and placed in the back of an unmarked vehicle by federal agents.[6] 345→

346→ 347→

January 23–24 — City of Austin Addresses ICE Rumors

348→ 349→

Do not open the door for ICE unless they show a judicial warrant signed by a judge.

Do not sign anything without speaking to a lawyer.

Do not carry a foreign passport — it can accelerate deportation.

Preparation is power — complete a family preparedness plan today.

Save your local rapid response hotline in your phone now.

Support Our Community

All donations go 100% to mutual aid through Gifted Dreamers, Inc., an Austin, Texas-based 501(c)(3).

Donate Now

Share a Resource or Send a Message

Only message is required. All other fields are optional.

1→# Contributing to justnice.us 2→ 3→Thank you for your interest in contributing to justnice.us. This site provides immigrant resources, Know Your Rights training guides, and articles on anti-surveillance technology. It is maintained by [Gifted Dreamers, Inc.](https://gifteddreamers.org), a Texas-based 501(c)(3) nonprofit. 4→ 5→Whether you write code, speak multiple languages, work in immigration law, or simply want to help keep information accurate, there is a place for you here. 6→ 7→--- 8→ 9→## Ways to Contribute 10→ 11→There are many ways to help that do not require writing code: 12→ 13→- **Report resource updates.** Phone numbers, URLs, and organizations change. If you notice outdated contact information or a broken link, open a [GitHub Issue](https://github.com/Gifted-Dreamers/justnice.us/issues) with the details. 14→- **Submit new resources.** Know of an organization, hotline, or legal aid provider that should be listed? Use the contact form on the site or open an issue describing the resource and why it should be included. 15→- **Fix broken links or outdated information.** If you can verify that a link is dead or a phone number has changed, let us know or submit a pull request with the correction. 16→- **Improve accessibility.** We aim for the site to be usable by everyone. If you find barriers related to screen readers, keyboard navigation, color contrast, or anything else, please report them. 17→- **Add translations.** Spanish translations are the highest priority, but all languages are welcome. If you can translate a page, open an issue to coordinate or submit a pull request. 18→- **Report bugs or suggest improvements.** Layout issues, typos, mobile rendering problems, or ideas for new features are all welcome as GitHub Issues. 19→- **Submit blog articles.** If you have expertise in anti-surveillance technology, digital privacy, immigration law, or related topics, we welcome article submissions. Open an issue with a brief pitch or draft. 20→ 21→--- 22→ 23→## How to Contribute Code 24→ 25→This is a static HTML site with no build step. Contributing is straightforward. 26→ 27→1. **Fork the repository.** Go to [github.com/Gifted-Dreamers/justnice.us](https://github.com/Gifted-Dreamers/justnice.us) and click Fork. 28→ 29→2. **Clone your fork and create a feature branch.** 30→ ```bash 31→ git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/justnice.us.git 32→ cd justnice.us 33→ git checkout -b your-branch-name 34→ ``` 35→ 36→3. **Make your changes.** Edit HTML files directly. There is no build step, no package manager, and no framework to configure. 37→ 38→4. **Test locally.** From the project root, start a local server: 39→ ```bash 40→ python3 -m http.server 8080 41→ ``` 42→ Then open `http://localhost:8080` in your browser. Check your changes on both desktop and mobile screen sizes. 43→ 44→5. **Commit and push.** 45→ ```bash 46→ git add -A 47→ git commit -m "Brief description of changes" 48→ git push origin your-branch-name 49→ ``` 50→ 51→6. **Open a pull request.** Go to your fork on GitHub and click "Compare & pull request." Provide a clear description of what you changed and why. 52→ 53→Deployments happen automatically when changes are merged to `main` via GitHub Actions. 54→ 55→--- 56→ 57→## Coding Guidelines 58→ 59→- **Pure static HTML.** Do not introduce JavaScript frameworks, bundlers, or build tools. The site uses no npm, no React, and no compiled assets. 60→- **Tailwind CSS via CDN.** Styles are applied using Tailwind utility classes loaded from the CDN. Do not add compiled CSS files or a local Tailwind build. 61→- **Color scheme.** Follow the existing palette: 62→ - Maroon: `#8B1A2B` 63→ - Teal: `#0097A7` 64→ - Gold: `#F5A623` 65→- **Phone numbers.** All phone numbers must be wrapped in click-to-call links: 66→ ```html 67→ (800) 555-1234 68→ ``` 69→- **External links.** All links to external sites must include safety attributes: 70→ ```html 71→ Example 72→ ``` 73→- **Responsive design.** Use a mobile-first approach. Test at small screen sizes before large ones. 74→- **Accessibility.** Use semantic HTML elements (`nav`, `main`, `section`, `article`, `header`, `footer`). Include ARIA labels where appropriate. Ensure all interactive elements are reachable via keyboard navigation. Provide meaningful `alt` text for images. 75→ 76→--- 77→ 78→## Content Guidelines 79→ 80→- **Verify resources.** Any organization, hotline, or service listed on the site must be verified as currently active before submission. Include a source or link demonstrating the resource is operational. 81→- **No personally identifiable information.** Do not include names, addresses, case numbers, or any other information that could identify community members. 82→- **Verify hotline numbers.** Phone numbers for hotlines and legal aid must be confirmed as current. If you are updating a number, note how you verified it. 83→- **Cite legal sources.** Any legal information or guidance must cite its source (statute, regulation, official guidance document, or qualified legal organization). 84→ 85→--- 86→ 87→## Code of Conduct 88→ 89→We expect all contributors to communicate respectfully. This project serves vulnerable communities, and maintaining a safe, welcoming environment is essential. Harassment, discrimination, and hostile behavior will not be tolerated. Maintainers reserve the right to remove comments, close issues, or block contributors who do not uphold these standards. 90→ 91→--- 92→ 93→## Questions? 94→ 95→If you are unsure about anything, have a question, or want to discuss an idea before submitting a pull request: 96→ 97→- Open a [GitHub Issue](https://github.com/Gifted-Dreamers/justnice.us/issues) 98→- Use the contact form on [justnice.us](https://justnice.us) 99→ 100→We appreciate every contribution, large or small. Thank you for helping keep these resources accurate and accessible. 101→

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en" class="scroll-smooth">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>ICE Activity Near Austin, TX (Jan 10 – Feb 10, 2026) — justNICE</title>

  <!-- SEO Meta -->
  <meta name="description" content="A comprehensive, sourced timeline of ICE enforcement operations within a 2-hour radius of Austin, Texas from January 10 to February 10, 2026, including community responses and policy changes.">
  <meta name="keywords" content="ICE, immigration enforcement, Austin Texas, Travis County, Hays County, deportation, community response, SB 8, 287g, immigration raids, know your rights">

  <!-- Open Graph -->
  <meta property="og:title" content="ICE Activity Near Austin, TX (Jan 10 – Feb 10, 2026) — justNICE">
  <meta property="og:description" content="A comprehensive, sourced timeline of ICE enforcement operations within a 2-hour radius of Austin, Texas from January 10 to February 10, 2026.">
  <meta property="og:url" content="https://justnice.us/blog/atx-ice-activity.html">
  <meta property="og:type" content="article">
  <meta property="og:image" content="https://justnice.us/blog/hero-atx-ice-activity.jpg">
  <meta property="article:published_time" content="2026-02-10">

  <!-- Twitter Card -->
  <meta name="twitter:card" content="summary_large_image">
  <meta name="twitter:title" content="ICE Activity Near Austin, TX (Jan 10 – Feb 10, 2026) — justNICE">
  <meta name="twitter:description" content="A comprehensive, sourced timeline of ICE enforcement operations within a 2-hour radius of Austin, Texas from January 10 to February 10, 2026.">
  <meta name="twitter:image" content="https://justnice.us/blog/hero-atx-ice-activity.jpg">

  <!-- Favicon -->
  <link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="32x32" href="/assets/favicon-32.png">

  <!-- DNS Prefetch -->

  <!-- Tailwind CDN -->
  <script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com/3.4.17"></script>
  <script>
    tailwind.config = {
      theme: {
        extend: {
          colors: {
            maroon: '#8B1A2B',
            gold: '#F5A623',
          },
          fontFamily: {
            sans: ['Inter', 'system-ui', 'sans-serif'],
          }
        }
      }
    }
  </script>

  <!-- Google Fonts: Inter (async) -->
  <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
  <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>
  <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@300;400;500;600;700;800&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">

  <!-- Custom styles for article content -->
  <style>
    /* Tables */
    .content-area table {
      width: 100%;
      border-collapse: collapse;
      margin: 1.5rem 0;
      font-size: 0.95rem;
    }
    .content-area table thead th {
      background-color: #8B1A2B;
      color: #fff;
      padding: 0.75rem 1rem;
      text-align: left;
      font-weight: 600;
    }
    .content-area table tbody td {
      padding: 0.75rem 1rem;
      border-bottom: 1px solid #e5e7eb;
    }
    .content-area table tbody tr:nth-child(even) {
      background-color: #fdf2f4;
    }
    .content-area table tbody tr:hover {
      background-color: #fce7eb;
    }

    /* Blockquotes */

294→ ICE Activity Within a 2-Hour Radius of Austin, TX 295→

296→

297→ The past 30 days have seen a significant escalation of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations in and around Austin, Texas, accompanied by community protests, policy changes, and legal developments. Below is a comprehensive timeline of reported incidents from January 10 to February 10, 2026, organized by location and date, with all sources cited. 298→

299→
300→ 307→ 308→ 309→ 310→
311→ 312→
313→ 314→ 315→

Austin / Travis County

316→ 317→

January 5 — Deportation After 911 Call

318→ 319→

320→ Austin Police responded to a disturbance call on Blue Stem Trail. The woman who called, Karen Gutiérrez-Castellanos, a Honduran national, was found to have an ICE administrative warrant in the database. APD notified ICE, which took custody of Gutiérrez-Castellanos and her 5-year-old daughter — a U.S. citizen. Both were deported to Honduras within days, without an opportunity to speak with an attorney or appear before a judge. The incident became a catalyst for major policy changes at APD and citywide outrage.[1][2][3] 321→

322→ 323→

January 12–13 — ICE Chase on SH 130 and Confusion at Travis County Sheriff’s Office

324→ 325→

326→ Federal and state officers pursuing suspects on SH 130 south of Austin called for assistance from the Travis County Sheriff’s Office. Due to confusion about the nature of the request and communications involving Pflugerville PD, a TCSO supervisor initially declined to assist. The chase ended on Pearce Lane near the Sun Chase subdivision, northeast of Circuit of the Americas. A separate, uninvolved motorist was involved in a crash. Sheriff Sally Hernandez issued a statement saying TCSO’s policy is not to decline assistance to other law enforcement agencies and that “recent events on the national stage” may have affected the response.[4] 327→

328→ 329→

January 13 — Incident Near Hutto (Williamson County)

330→ 331→

332→ On the same day, Williamson County deputies responded around 5 a.m. to reports of gunshots fired from a car on Highway 79 near Hutto. Four occupants, identified as undocumented migrants, were taken into custody. One was jailed on charges related to the shooting, and the other three were turned over to ICE.[4] 333→

334→ 335→

January 14 — APD Announces Policy Change on ICE Cooperation

336→ 337→

338→ APD Chief Lisa Davis announced the department would update its “general orders” to explicitly allow officers to cooperate more fully with federal immigration officers in executing immigration warrants. Under the new policy, officers who decide to report someone to ICE must first notify a duty commander. Davis stated, “Senate Bill 4 does not allow us to tell officers they cannot call ICE on these things.” Multiple Austin City Council members said they do not anticipate this leading to more cooperation in practice.[5][1] 339→

340→ 341→

January 18 — ICE Activity on SH 195, Williamson County

342→ 343→

344→ ICE activity was spotted along State Highway 195 in Williamson County, north of Austin. A KXAN reporter described witnessing a traffic stop in which a person was handcuffed and placed in the back of an unmarked vehicle by federal agents.[6] 345→

346→ 347→

January 23–24 — City of Austin Addresses ICE Rumors

348→ 349→

Community & Policy February 10, 2026

ICE Activity Within a 2-Hour Radius of Austin, TX

The past 30 days have seen a significant escalation of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations in and around Austin, Texas, accompanied by community protests, policy changes, and legal developments. Below is a comprehensive timeline of reported incidents from January 10 to February 10, 2026, organized by location and date, with all sources cited.

Austin / Travis County

January 5 — Deportation After 911 Call

Austin Police responded to a disturbance call on Blue Stem Trail. The woman who called, Karen Gutiérrez-Castellanos, a Honduran national, was found to have an ICE administrative warrant in the database. APD notified ICE, which took custody of Gutiérrez-Castellanos and her 5-year-old daughter — a U.S. citizen. Both were deported to Honduras within days, without an opportunity to speak with an attorney or appear before a judge. The incident became a catalyst for major policy changes at APD and citywide outrage.[1][2][3]

January 12–13 — ICE Chase on SH 130 and Confusion at Travis County Sheriff’s Office

Federal and state officers pursuing suspects on SH 130 south of Austin called for assistance from the Travis County Sheriff’s Office. Due to confusion about the nature of the request and communications involving Pflugerville PD, a TCSO supervisor initially declined to assist. The chase ended on Pearce Lane near the Sun Chase subdivision, northeast of Circuit of the Americas. A separate, uninvolved motorist was involved in a crash. Sheriff Sally Hernandez issued a statement saying TCSO’s policy is not to decline assistance to other law enforcement agencies and that “recent events on the national stage” may have affected the response.[4]

January 13 — Incident Near Hutto (Williamson County)

On the same day, Williamson County deputies responded around 5 a.m. to reports of gunshots fired from a car on Highway 79 near Hutto. Four occupants, identified as undocumented migrants, were taken into custody. One was jailed on charges related to the shooting, and the other three were turned over to ICE.[4]

January 14 — APD Announces Policy Change on ICE Cooperation

APD Chief Lisa Davis announced the department would update its “general orders” to explicitly allow officers to cooperate more fully with federal immigration officers in executing immigration warrants. Under the new policy, officers who decide to report someone to ICE must first notify a duty commander. Davis stated, “Senate Bill 4 does not allow us to tell officers they cannot call ICE on these things.” Multiple Austin City Council members said they do not anticipate this leading to more cooperation in practice.[5][1]

January 18 — ICE Activity on SH 195, Williamson County

ICE activity was spotted along State Highway 195 in Williamson County, north of Austin. A KXAN reporter described witnessing a traffic stop in which a person was handcuffed and placed in the back of an unmarked vehicle by federal agents.[6]

January 23–24 — City of Austin Addresses ICE Rumors

Research ICE enforcement activity in Texas statewide for January-March 2026. I need:

1. **SB 8 (Texas immigration law)** — what it requires, enforcement deadline, which counties have complied

6. **Construction/agricultural industry impact** — worksite raids, economic effects
7. **Deportation flights** — flights from Texas airports (San Antonio, El Paso, etc.)
8. **Legal challenges** — any federal court rulings affecting Texas immigration enforcement
9. **Sanctuary city status** — which Texas cities still resist ICE cooperation

Provide specific dates, numbers, locations, and source URLs for everything. Label RGV and El Paso sections clearly.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/kristinesocall/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/frontend-design/205b6e0b3036/skills/frontend-design

This skill guides creation of distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces that avoid generic "AI slop" aesthetics. Implement real working code with exceptional attention to aesthetic details and creative choices.

The user provides frontend requirements: a component, page, application, or interface to build. They may include context about the purpose, audience, or technical constraints.

## Design Thinking

Before coding, understand the context and commit to a BOLD aesthetic direction:
- **Purpose**: What problem does this interface solve? Who uses it?
- **Tone**: Pick an extreme: brutally minimal, maximalist chaos, retro-futuristic, organic/natural, luxury/refined, playful/toy-like, editorial/magazine, brutalist/raw, art deco/geometric, soft/pastel, industrial/utilitarian, etc. There are so many flavors to choose from. Use these for inspiration but design one that is true to the aesthetic direction.
- **Constraints**: Technical requirements (framework, performance, accessibility).
- **Differentiation**: What makes this UNFORGETTABLE? What's the one thing someone will remember?

**CRITICAL**: Choose a clear conceptual direction and execute it with precision. Bold maximalism and refined minimalism both work - the key is intentionality, not intensity.

Then implement working code (HTML/CSS/JS, React, Vue, etc.) that is:
- Production-grade and functional
- Visually striking and memorable
- Cohesive with a clear aesthetic point-of-view
- Meticulously refined in every detail

## Frontend Aesthetics Guidelines

Focus on:
- **Typography**: Choose fonts that are beautiful, unique, and interesting. Avoid generic fonts like Arial and Inter; opt instead for distinctive choices that elevate the frontend's aesthetics; unexpected, characterful font choices. Pair a distinctive display font with a refined body font.
- **Color & Theme**: Commit to a cohesive aesthetic. Use CSS variables for consistency. Dominant colors with sharp accents outperform timid, evenly-distributed palettes.
- **Motion**: Use animations for effects and micro-interactions. Prioritize CSS-only solutions for HTML. Use Motion library for React when available. Focus on high-impact moments: one well-orchestrated page load with staggered reveals (animation-delay) creates more delight than scattered micro-interactions. Use scroll-triggering and hover states that surprise.
- **Spatial Composition**: Unexpected layouts. Asymmetry. Overlap. Diagonal flow. Grid-breaking elements. Generous negative space OR controlled density.
- **Backgrounds & Visual Details**: Create atmosphere and depth rather than defaulting to solid colors. Add contextual effects and textures that match the overall aesthetic. Apply creative forms like gradient meshes, noise textures, geometric patterns, layered transparencies, dramatic shadows, decorative borders, custom cursors, and grain overlays.

NEVER use generic AI-generated aesthetics like overused font families (Inter, Roboto, Arial, system fonts), cliched color schemes (particularly purple gradients on white backgrounds), predictable layouts and component patterns, and cookie-cutter design that lacks context-specific character.

Interpret creatively and make unexpected choices that feel genuinely designed for the context. No design should be the same. Vary between light and dark themes, different fonts, different aesthetics. NEVER converge on common choices (Space Grotesk, for example) across generations.

**IMPORTANT**: Match implementation complexity to the aesthetic vision. Maximalist designs need elaborate code with extensive animations and effects. Minimalist or refined designs need restraint, precision, and careful attention to spacing, typography, and subtle details. Elegance comes from executing the vision well.

Remember: Claude is capable of extraordinary creative work. Don't hold back, show what can truly be created when thinking outside the box and committing fully to a distinctive vision.

<result>Here is a comprehensive research report on ICE enforcement activity in San Antonio, Texas for January-March 2026.

---

# ICE Enforcement Activity in San Antonio, TX -- January-March 2026

## 1. City Council Meeting on ICE (January 22, 2026)

San Antonio City Council held a special session on **January 22, 2026** to address increased ICE presence in the city. The meeting ran approximately **7 hours**, with public comments alone spanning over 5 hours. Approximately **180 people signed up to speak**, with the vast majority opposing any cooperation between the city and ICE. The meeting was recessed multiple times due to disruptions from the audience.

**Key outcomes and statements:**
- Police Chief **William McManus** emphasized that SAPD does not enforce immigration law and does not arrest people for immigration violations. He cited data showing SAPD made roughly **51,000 arrests** the prior year, of which only **111 involved ICE detainer requests**.
- District 5 Councilwoman **Teri Castillo** called ICE's presence "textbook fascism" and said "ICE doesn't belong here."
- Councilman **Marc Whyte** took the opposing position, stating SAPD must comply with federal law when called upon.
- Mayor **Gina Ortiz Jones** framed the session as offering residents "a transparent dialogue" about legal guidelines shaping city-federal collaboration.

**Subsequent council actions:**
- **February 12, 2026**: Council approved a resolution directing staff to explore interventions and impacts of federal immigration enforcement, including options to fight the East Side ICE facility.
- **March 5, 2026**: Council voted **8-2** to explore a moratorium and zoning limits on private detention facilities. Councilman **Jalen McKee-Rodriguez** added an amendment for a potential moratorium on creation of detention centers. However, Deputy City Attorney **Susan Guinn** confirmed that neither zoning nor the moratorium would affect the existing East Side ICE facility, since federal property is exempt from local zoning.

Sources:
- [TPR: Immigration enforcement debate prompts disruptions at lengthy San Antonio City Council meeting](https://www.tpr.org/news/2026-01-22/san-antonio-city-council-meeting-over-ice-cooperation-grows-contentious)
- [KSAT: Public comments span 5 hours at San Antonio City Council meeting on ICE cooperation](https://www.ksat.com/news/local/2026/01/23/public-comments-span-more-than-5-hours-at-san-antono-city-council-meeting-on-ice-cooperation/)
- [KENS5: Most of SA City Council calls for transparency, decries federal agency's operations](https://www.kens5.com/article/news/local/san-antonio-city-council-ice-meeting-protest-police-immigration/273-3c736283-3c53-428f-80dd-5261dde77805)
- [TPR: San Antonio City Council votes to explore moratorium, zoning limits on detention facilities](https://www.tpr.org/government-politics/2026-03-05/san-antonio-city-council-votes-to-explore-moratorium-zoning-limits-on-detention-facilities)
- [SA Current: San Antonio City Council votes 8-2 to explore moratorium](https://www.sacurrent.com/news/texas-news/san-antonio-city-council-votes-8-2-to-explore-moratorium-on-new-detention-centers/)

---

## 2. $66 Million Warehouse Purchase

The federal government purchased a **640,000-square-foot warehouse** at **542 SE Loop 410** on San Antonio's East Side for **$66 million**. The facility was built in 2022 and was previously described as San Antonio's largest industrial complex. The Bexar Appraisal District valued it at over **$37 million**.

**Purpose:** ICE confirmed it will function as a **processing facility with up to 1,500 beds** -- not a large-scale long-term detention center but rather a staging/processing site for immigrants who have been arrested and are awaiting processing.

**Timeline:** A federal planning document shows the facility is expected to be **operational by November 30, 2026**. The purchase is part of a broader national build-out funded by the "One Big Beautiful Bill," which aims to support tens of thousands of additional detention beds.

**Opposition:** Congressman **Henry Cuellar** called the purchase "not a responsible use of taxpayer dollars." City officials explored whether zoning authority or environmental studies could block the sale but ultimately acknowledged ICE does not need local approval to buy property.

Sources:
- [News4SA: ICE purchases East Side mega warehouse for detention center](https://news4sanantonio.com/news/investigations/ice-purchases-huge-warehouse-on-east-side-for-detention-center)
- [KSAT: ICE confirms purchase of East Side warehouse](https://www.ksat.com/news/local/2026/02/05/ice-purchases-east-side-facility-to-hold-immigrant-detainees-agency-confirms/)
- [San Antonio Report: ICE detention facility will be active by late 2026](https://sanantonioreport.org/federal-documents-show-ice-detention-facility-in-san-antonio-will-be-open-by-november/)
- [KENS5: Congressman Cuellar criticizes ICE facility](https://www.kens5.com/article/news/local/henry-cuellar-criticizes-ice-facility-san-antonio-texas-immigration-congress/273-c16d8bdf-bbdd-4716-9993-e46fdbfe3e6c)

---

## 3. Vehicle Ramming Incident (January 13, 2026)

On **January 13, 2026**, ICE arrested **Robyn Argote Brooks** (also referred to as Argote-Brooks), a Cuban national, after he rammed two ICE vehicles in a **Walmart parking lot in the 12600 block of Blanco Road** (North Side of San Antonio).

**What happened:** ICE officers were conducting a targeted vehicle stop. Argote-Brooks refused to exit his vehicle, threw it into reverse at full speed (nearly running over an officer), slammed into one ICE vehicle, then accelerated forward into a second ICE vehicle, **injuring the officer in the front seat**. The officer was hospitalized.

**Charges:** Destruction of government property. Damage to one vehicle was assessed at **$4,847.18**.

**Dispute over legal status:** Argote-Brooks' father claimed his son was in the U.S. legally. DHS described him as an "illegal alien." On **January 30**, a judge ordered Argote-Brooks to remain in custody.

**Broader context:** DHS reported that from Jan. 21, 2025 through Jan. 7, 2026, ICE officers experienced **66 vehicular attacks** -- a **3,200% increase** compared to just two during the same prior-year period. By February 3, that number had risen to over **180 vehicle attacks** nationally.

Sources:
- [DHS: ICE Arrests Illegal Alien Who Weaponized His Vehicle](https://www.dhs.gov/news/2026/01/15/ice-arrests-illegal-alien-who-weaponized-his-vehicle-ramming-attack-law-enforcement)
- [KSAT: Immigrant rams into ICE vehicles in San Antonio](https://www.ksat.com/news/local/2026/01/14/immigrant-rams-into-ice-vehicles-in-san-antonio-injuring-officer-agency-says/)
- [KSAT: Man charged after ramming ICE vehicles was in the US legally, father says](https://www.ksat.com/news/local/2026/01/20/man-charged-after-ramming-ice-vehicles-on-north-side-was-in-the-us-legally-father-says/)

---

## 4. Home Raids in San Antonio Area

**Viral home raid (early February 2026):** ICE officers followed an immigrant inside his home during an arrest attempt, captured on video that went viral with nearly **3 million views**. Two Honduran nationals were arrested -- **Marcos Joel Contreras-Max** and **Edwin Javier Chinchilla-Lopez** -- both charged with illegal re-entry after previous deportation.

**Key controversies:**
- The raid was launched under the stated goal of "disrupting Tren de Aragua" operations, but the state search-warrant affidavit outlined a far narrower case built primarily on a **single confidential informant**.
- Congressman **Joaquin Castro** called for an investigation after video showed ICE agents **smashing security cameras**.
- Castro noted that **92 people were detained and charged with nothing**, including "two teenage boys who were grabbing something to eat."

**Food truck area raid (November 2025, but aftermath continued into 2026):** A major pre-dawn operation near **San Pedro Avenue and Basse Road** resulted in over **140 undocumented individuals** taken into ICE custody, including **51 confirmed Tren de Aragua members**. Video showed people hiding inside food trucks and an agent destroying a surveillance camera belonging to the food truck "Las Pollitas Snack y Taco."

Sources:
- [KSAT: DHS says ICE was attempting to arrest immigrant in viral San Antonio home raid video](https://www.ksat.com/news/local/2026/02/07/dhs-says-ice-was-attempting-to-arrest-immigrant-assailant-in-viral-san-antonio-home-raid-video/)
- [News4SA: Castro calls for probe of alleged ICE home raid](https://news4sanantonio.com/news/local/castro-investigates-alleged-ice-raid-on-san-antonio-home-after-video-surfaces-texas-bexar-county-investgation-immigration-crisis)
- [San Antonio Report: Leaders demand answers on San Antonio immigration raid](https://sanantonioreport.org/san-antonio-leaders-demand-answers-on-evidence-behind-major-raid/)
- [KENS5: Chaos in a San Antonio ICE raid shows people hiding in food trucks](https://www.kens5.com/article/news/community/texas-video-san-antonio-ice-raid-food-trucks-agents-breaking-security-camera/273-11150acd-7a3d-4e4d-8120-b512ae835c21)

---

## 5. ICE Operations Near Bexar County Courthouse

ICE agents arrested immigrants **outside the federal immigration courthouse in downtown San Antonio** after they appeared for **scheduled immigration court hearings**. A U.S. Senator (video documented) criticized ICE activity at the San Antonio Immigration Court.

This pattern of courthouse arrests has drawn significant criticism as it deters immigrants from attending their own court proceedings.

Sources:
- [KSAT: US senator criticizes ICE activity at San Antonio Immigration Court in video](https://www.ksat.com/news/local/2026/01/24/us-senator-criticizes-ice-activity-at-san-antonio-immigration-court-in-video/)
- [News4SA: Are San Antonio courts a target for immigration enforcement operations?](https://news4sanantonio.com/news/local/are-san-antonio-courts-a-target-for-immigration-enforcement-operations-bexar-county-sheriffs-office-san-antonio-police-department)

---

## 6. Military Base Involvement

**Joint Base San Antonio-Lackland** has been designated as part of a **National Defense Area** for migrant detention.

**Key details:**
- The Trump administration declared Lackland and surrounding areas as "National Defense Areas," considered extensions of existing military installations.
- The zone covers approximately **250 miles along the Rio Grande** in Cameron and Hidalgo counties.
- The plan supports the administration's goal of **detaining 3,000 migrants daily**.
- Troops will have designated spaces to temporarily detain migrants before transferring them to law enforcement authorities.
- Experts confirmed that using the military in support roles is legal, as long as active service members are not making initial arrests.
- The South Texas ICE Processing Center in **Pearsall** (near San Antonio) is approaching capacity, and the South Texas Family Regional Detention Center in **Dilley** (the nation's largest) has recently reopened.
- This echoes **2014**, when Lackland previously housed migrants during deportation processing.

Sources:
- [SA Current: San Antonio's Joint Base-Lackland to detain immigrants awaiting deportation](https://www.sacurrent.com/news/san-antonios-joint-base-lackland-to-detain-immigrants-awaiting-deportation-37880506/)
- [News4SA: Questions arise after Lackland declared new military defense zone for migrant detention](https://news4sanantonio.com/news/local/questions-arise-after-lackland-declared-new-military-defense-zone-for-migrant-detention-joint-base-san-antonio-air-force-us-trump-administration)
- [KRIS-TV: Questions arise after Lackland AFB declared new military defense zone](https://www.kristv.com/news/texas-news/questions-arise-after-lackland-afb-in-san-antonio-declared-new-military-defense-zone-for-migrant-detention)

---

## 7. Deportation Flights from San Antonio Airport

San Antonio is one of **five ICE Air Operations locations** in the United States, making the city's airport a hub for deportation flights. DHS purchased **six Boeing 737s** in December 2025 to build its own fleet. The planned 1,500-bed East Side facility and Lackland's detention role both feed into San Antonio's function as a deportation logistics hub.

Sources:
- [ICE Air Operations](https://www.ice.gov/factsheets/ice-air-operations)
- [Human Rights First: ICE Flight Monitor January 2026 Report](https://humanrightsfirst.org/library/ice-flight-monitor-january-2026-report/)
- [San Antonio Report: ICE detention facility in San Antonio will be active by late 2026](https://sanantonioreport.org/federal-documents-show-ice-detention-facility-in-san-antonio-will-be-open-by-november/)

---

## 8. Local Law Enforcement Cooperation Level

The picture is complex and fragmented across agencies:

**SAPD (San Antonio Police Department):**
- Chief McManus maintains SAPD does **not** enforce immigration law and does not arrest people for immigration violations.
- However, under Texas **Senate Bill 4** (passed 2017), SAPD is required to report anyone not legally in the country to ICE if already in custody on a different charge.
- Of ~51,000 arrests, only **111 involved ICE detainer requests**.

**Bexar County Sheriff's Office:**
- Entered into a **287(g) Warrant Service Officer agreement** with ICE in late October 2025. This is the most **limited form** of cooperation -- it applies only to people already in custody at the county jail.
- Sheriff **Javier Salazar** said he signed early so the community could see what participation looks like.

**More expansive partnerships:**
- The **Texas Department of Public Safety** and the **Precinct 3 Constable's Office** adopted the most expansive version of the 287(g) program, authorizing trained officers to assist with immigration enforcement **in the field**.
- Under a new **Texas ICE Law**, most county sheriffs must enter 287(g) agreements by **December 1, 2026**.

Sources:
- [San Antonio Report: San Antonio police says it must cooperate with ICE under state law](https://sanantonioreport.org/san-antonio-police-enforce-immigration-laws-ice-city-council/)
- [SA Current: San Antonio Police chief clarifies department's cooperation with ICE](https://www.sacurrent.com/news/san-antonio-news/san-antonio-police-chief-clarifies-departments-cooperation-with-ice-during-chaotic-council-meeting/)
- [San Antonio Report: Bexar County agencies opt into ICE partnership](https://sanantonioreport.org/bexar-county-precinct-balcones-heights-police-opt-into-immigration-enforcement/)
- [Axios San Antonio: Here's how San Antonio police do and don't work with ICE](https://www.axios.com/local/san-antonio/2026/01/27/san-antonio-city-council-ice-immigration-police)

---

## 9. Community Response, Protests, Legal Clinics

**Protests:**
- **January 11, 2026**: Early march in San Antonio; protesters said "Things are getting very scary."
- **January 21, 2026**: Dozens protested on Trump's one-year mark in office.
- **January 30, 2026**: **Hundreds** marched to **Travis Park** in downtown San Antonio as part of a **nationwide day of action** against ICE, calling for reform and abolishment.
- Multiple disruptions during the January 22 city council meeting.

**Council actions toward community support:**
- The city is considering a **justice fund** to support nonprofits and people affected by ICE enforcement with legal services.
- City staff advised that direct city-led "Know Your Rights" advisories during police interactions carry significant legal risk and were not recommended.

**Labor response:**
- The **Texas AFL-CIO** passed resolutions at their 2026 COPE Convention supporting immigrants and calling for ICE out of communities.

**Bexar County Judge Peter Sakai** compared ICE raids to **Japanese American internment camps** (January 24, 2026).

Sources:
- [KSAT: San Antonio immigration protest draws hundreds to Travis Park](https://www.ksat.com/news/local/2026/01/30/hundreds-marching-in-downtown-san-antonio-as-part-of-nationwide-protest-against-ice/)
- [TPR: Things are getting very scary - Protesters march in San Antonio](https://www.tpr.org/government-politics/2026-01-11/things-are-getting-very-scary-protesters-march-in-san-antonio-against-ice-actions)
- [KSAT: Dozens protest in San Antonio on Trump's one-year mark](https://www.ksat.com/news/local/2026/01/21/dozens-protest-in-san-antonio-against-immigration-enforcement-on-trumps-one-year-mark-in-office/)
- [KSAT: Bexar County Judge Sakai compares ICE raids to Japanese American internment](https://www.ksat.com/news/local/2026/01/24/bexar-county-judge-peter-sakai-compares-recent-ice-raids-to-japanese-american-internment-camps/)
- [Texas AFL-CIO: Delegates pass resolutions supporting immigrants](https://texasaflcio.org/news/texas-afl-cio-delegates-pass-resolutions-supporting-immigrants-calling-ice-out)

---

## 10. Worksite Raids and Business Impacts

**Construction industry:**
- ICE has been raiding **construction sites along the border** and in urban areas. The **South Texas Builders Association** documented ICE agents speeding through subdivisions in unmarked vehicles without presenting warrants.
- An **Associated General Contractors of America** survey found **92% of construction businesses** are struggling to fill positions amid ICE raids, with **28% of firms directly affected** over a six-month period.
- Mario Guerrero (South Texas Builders Association) stated: "There has to be some sort of warrant that needs to be presented when these federal agents show up to construction sites. And there's no warrants being presented."

**ICE at RGV worksites:**
- ICE's Rio Grande Valley office announced **12 arrested in a South Texas worksite enforcement operation** with federal partners.

**Uncertainty about major events:**
- ICE's San Antonio Field Office **refused to say** whether enforcement operations would target the **San Antonio Stock Show & Rodeo** or **Fiesta** celebrations. Spokeswoman **Nina Pruneda** said she would "look into" the matter but the agency never responded to follow-ups, creating a chilling effect on attendance at major community events.

Sources:
- [SA Current: South Texas construction group accuses ICE of warrantless raids](https://www.sacurrent.com/news/texas-news/south-texas-construction-group-accuses-ice-of-warrantless-raids-endangering-neighborhoods/)
- [Hoodline: ICE Won't Say If It Plans Raids At San Antonio Rodeo, Fiesta](https://hoodline.com/2026/02/ice-silence-spurs-jitters-over-possible-raids-at-san-antonio-rodeo-and-fiesta/)
- [SA Current: ICE won't say whether it's targeting San Antonio Rodeo, Fiesta](https://www.sacurrent.com/news/ice-wont-say-whether-its-targeting-san-antonio-rodeo-fiesta-for-enforcement-actions/)
- [AInvest: Assessing the Economic Impact of Aggressive ICE Raids in South Texas](https://www.ainvest.com/news/assessing-economic-impact-aggressive-ice-raids-south-texas-2602/)
- [ICE.gov: 12 arrested in South Texas worksite enforcement operation](https://www.ice.gov/news/releases/12-arrested-south-texas-worksite-enforcement-operation-ice-rio-grande-valley-federal)

---

<result>Here is a comprehensive research report on ICE enforcement activity in Dallas-Fort Worth for the period January-March 2026.

---

## 1. Daily Arrest Numbers at DFW Field Office

**84 arrests in a single day (January 26, 2025):** ICE confirmed 84 arrests across Dallas, Fort Worth, Arlington, Irving, Garland, and Collin County during a single Sunday operation. This was among the first major weekend sweeps following the inauguration.
- Source: [WFAA - ICE arrests 84 people in North Texas](https://www.wfaa.com/article/news/local/ice-arrests-84-people-in-north-texas-during-immigration-enforcement-operations-agency-says/287-1b720e88-51a1-4060-9abc-fa6fd297233a)
- Source: [NBC 5 DFW](https://www.nbcdfw.com/investigations/ice-arrests-dozens-dallas-targeted-operation/3751649/)
- Source: [CBS Texas](https://www.cbsnews.com/texas/news/arrests-north-texas-ice-raids/)

**75 daily arrest target (January 2025):** An internal ICE call set a goal of approximately 75 daily arrests per field office, including Dallas.
- Source: [FOX 4 DFW - Dallas ICE office told to aim for 75 daily arrests](https://www.fox4news.com/news/dallas-ice-raids)
- Source: [Dallas Express](https://dallasexpress.com/city/ice-sets-daily-target-for-dallas-arrests-at-75/)

**100 arrests/day (by late 2025-2026):** The Dallas field office reported averaging approximately 100 arrests per day across North Texas and Oklahoma, making it the **second-highest arrest total in the country**. Since October 2025, the office arrested more than 9,644 noncitizens.
- Source: [NBC 5 DFW - Dallas ICE ERO says it makes the second highest number of arrests in the country](https://www.nbcdfw.com/news/local/dallas-ice-ero-says-it-makes-the-second-highest-number-of-arrests-in-the-country/3969068/)

**108% year-over-year increase:** Dallas ICE agents arrested about 12,100 people between Jan. 20 and Oct. 16, 2025, a 108% increase compared to 2024. Notably, the share of arrestees **without criminal convictions** rose from 44% in 2024 to 62%.
- Source: [Hoodline - Dallas ICE Arrests Soar 108%](https://hoodline.com/2026/01/dallas-ice-arrests-soar-108-as-majority-nabbed-had-no-criminal-convictions/)
- Source: [Axios Dallas - Most Dallas ICE detainees didn't have convictions](https://www.axios.com/local/dallas/2026/01/16/dallas-ice-detainees-convictions-deportations)

**Statewide context:** Daily ICE arrests in Texas jumped from an average of 85/day (final 18 months of Biden) to 176/day under Trump. From January-July 2025, ICE made approximately 138,000 arrests nationwide, nearly a quarter in Texas.
- Source: [KERA News - As immigration officials ramp up migrant arrests](https://www.keranews.org/immigration/2026-01-28/immigration-arrests-quotas-north-texas-dallas-ice-tactics)

---

## 2. Probation Office Arrests

**Dallas County probation offices:** ICE agents entered Dallas County Community Supervision and Corrections Department (CSCD) offices to arrest individuals during routine check-ins. ICE used **administrative warrants** (not signed by a judge) with consent from the probation office.

**Commissioner Elba Garcia's response (November 2025):** Dallas County Commissioner Elba Garcia raised the alarm, stating that CSCD "failed to follow due process by not requiring judicial warrants for ICE arrests." She said she was working with Commissioners Court colleagues to ensure due process and that county facilities are not used to compromise residents' constitutional rights.
- Source: [KERA News - Dallas County commissioner raises alarm over ICE arrests in probation offices](https://www.keranews.org/news/2025-11-14/dallas-county-commissioner-elba-garcia-raises-alarm-over-ice-arrests-in-probation-offices)
- Source: [Union-Bulletin - ICE agents now entering Dallas County probation offices](https://www.union-bulletin.com/news/national/ice-agents-now-entering-dallas-county-probation-offices-to-make-arrests/article_d76ecd02-45e6-5de4-8cd0-f2f36634860f.html)

**New tactics at check-ins:** Advocates and attorneys report that ICE began sending late-night messages to immigrants notifying them of last-minute check-ins, then arresting them upon arrival. This marks a change from prior practice where check-ins were routine and did not result in detention.
- Source: [KERA News - New tactics emerge](https://www.keranews.org/immigration/2026-01-28/immigration-arrests-quotas-north-texas-dallas-ice-tactics)
- Source: [Houston Public Media](https://www.houstonpublicmedia.org/articles/news/politics/immigration/2026/01/28/541911/immigration-arrests-quotas-north-texas-dallas-ice-tactics/)

## 3. Courthouse Arrests

ICE arrested immigrants at the **Dallas courthouse** as part of a broader pattern of detentions at courts and federal buildings (including immigration courts in San Antonio and federal buildings in El Paso).
- Source: [CBS Texas - ICE arrests immigrants at Dallas courthouse](https://www.cbsnews.com/texas/news/dallas-courthouse-ice-arrests-deportation-crackdown/)

**Policy change:** The Trump administration revoked the Biden-era "sensitive locations" policy that had previously prohibited arrests near courts, schools, and places of worship. A January 2026 memo from ICE Director Todd Lyons directed supervisors to issue administrative warrants in real time during stops if ICE has probable cause.
- Source: [NBC 5 DFW - ICE removes protections at sensitive locations](https://www.nbcdfw.com/news/local/ice-removes-protections-sensitive-locations-immigration/3749256/)

## 4. Worksite Raids

**Construction site raids (February 2026):** ICE raids at Texas construction sites are causing significant economic damage. Foreign-born noncitizens make up approximately **38.6% of the construction workforce** in the Houston area (Census Bureau). Key impacts:
- Construction loans in the region down approximately **30%** over the past year
- Companies in the supply chain reporting sharp revenue declines, with some filing for **bankruptcy**
- Delays spreading into commercial developments, municipal projects, and new housing starts
- Workers avoiding job sites even if they have documents, due to fear of enforcement
- Source: [Texas Standard - Construction site ICE raids hurting economy](https://www.texasstandard.org/stories/construction-site-ice-raids-hurting-economy-and-building-industry/)

- Source: [Reporting Texas - ICE Raids Disrupt Workforce](https://www.reportingtexas.com/ice-raids-disrupt-workforce-and-construction-projects-across-texas/)

Activists filed code complaints against the Dallas ICE field office at **8101 N. Stemmons Fwy** (a 50,827-sq-ft building built in the 1950s) over overcrowding. ## 6. Local Law Enforcement Cooperation

### Dallas PD: REJECTED 287(g)

**Chief Daniel Comeaux rejected a $25 million federal offer** (October 2025) to join the 287(g) program, saying the department needs to focus on 911 calls and violent crime. "We don't need that attention, we don't want to deal with that smoke." The Dallas City Council committee later sided with the chief, rejecting the proposal. Over 10 community groups rallied at City Hall in support.
- Source: [CBS Texas - Dallas police rejected $25 million](https://www.cbsnews.com/texas/news/dallas-police-department-ice-immigration-enforcement-offer-texas/)

Note: The **Dallas County Sheriff's Office** does notify ICE about immigrants in its custody who may have immigration violations, even though DPD does not participate.

### Tarrant County: APPROVED 287(g) expansion

**February 10, 2026:** Tarrant County Commissioners approved (3-2 party-line vote) applying for the **Sheriff Immigration Law Enforcement Grant Program** ($140,000) to support ICE cooperation. The Tarrant County Sheriff's Office had already been in the 287(g) program since 2017 under Sheriff Bill Waybourn. About 20 residents spoke against the measure at the meeting.
- Source: [Fort Worth Report - Tarrant County seeks state grant](https://fortworthreport.org/2026/02/10/tarrant-county-seeks-state-grant-to-support-ice-partnership/)

### Arlington: REJECTED

**February 2026:** Arlington Mayor Jim Ross stated the city will not partner with ICE. "Immigration, absent criminal behavior, is handled in a civil immigration court, and we simply do not have the wherewithal to be able to participate."
- Source: [KERA News - Arlington mayor won't consider partnering with ICE](https://www.keranews.org/news/2026-02-12/mayor-council-member-wont-consider-partnering-arlington-police-with-ice-for-enforcement-operations)

State law now **requires** Texas sheriffs to apply to the 287(g) program.

# ICE Enforcement in Houston / Harris County, Texas (January-March 2026)

## 1. The 646-Arrest Operation (Feb 23 - Mar 2, 2026)

Between **February 23 and March 2, 2026**, ICE conducted a week-long targeted enforcement operation in the Houston area resulting in **646 arrests**. The operation was conducted jointly with federal, state, and local law enforcement.

Breakdown of the 646 arrests:
- **543** were individuals charged or convicted of criminal offenses while unlawfully present
- **7** documented gang members
- **71** criminal arrest warrants executed
- **140** charged or convicted of aggravated felony or violent offense (homicide, aggravated assault, domestic violence)
- **34** charged or convicted of sex offenses or child sex offenses (aggravated sexual assault of a minor, child pornography, rape)
- Other offenses included firearms charges, drug possession, burglary, theft, and DWI

This was part of a broader pattern. Earlier large-scale Houston operations in 2025 included a May operation (500+ deported, 400+ arrested in one week) and an October operation (1,500 arrested over 10 days). Between January and mid-October 2025, ICE made over **17,500 arrests** across the Houston field office region (Southeast Texas to Waco).

Sources:
- [ICE Official Press Release: 543 criminal aliens, 7 gang members](https://www.ice.gov/news/releases/ice-arrests-543-criminal-aliens-7-gang-members-houston-area-during-week-long-multi)
- [Texas Scorecard: 646 arrests including gang members](https://texasscorecard.com/local/major-ice-operation-nets-646-illegal-alien-arrests-in-houston-including-gang-members/)
- [CW39: 646 undocumented Houston area immigrants in week-long raid](https://cw39.com/news/local/ice-arrests-646-undocumented-houston-area-immigrants-in-week-long-raid/)

## 2. Harris County Jail ICE Detainers and Sheriff Cooperation

**Texas Senate Bill 8 (SB 8)** took effect **January 1, 2026** and mandates that sheriffs in Texas counties with populations over 100,000 (including Harris County) must enter into a **287(g) agreement** with ICE. Under 287(g), selected deputies are trained to act as federal immigration agents within the jail, verify immigration status of inmates, and issue ICE detainers or administrative warrants.

Key timeline:
- Harris County previously ended its 287(g) agreement in **2017** under then-Sheriff Ed Gonzalez (the county had spent $675,000 on the program before ending it)
- SB 8 forces reinstatement
- **December 1, 2026** deadline: the state attorney general may sue sheriffs who fail to request or enter into ICE agreements

Sources:
- [Understanding Your Rights During ICE Interviews at Harris County Jail Under SB 8](https://www.attorneydaniels.com/post/understanding-your-rights-during-ice-interviews-at-harris-county-jail-under-sb-8)
- [Houston's Cooperation with ICE: What the Law Says](https://www.iowaparkleader.com/houstons-cooperation-with-ice-what-the-law-says-in-texas/)
- [Texas Observer: Legislature Mandates Sheriff-ICE Cooperation](https://www.texasobserver.org/legislature-sheriffs-ice-cooperation-jails-streets/)
- [Houston Public Media: Harris County would be required to collaborate with ICE](https://www.houstonpublicmedia.org/articles/news/politics/immigration/2025/03/20/516366/harris-county-would-be-required-to-collaborate-with-ice-if-a-new-bill-is-approved-by-the-texas-senate/)

## 3. HPD Policy Changes and the 1,000% Surge in ICE Calls

**Official HPD Policy (since February 2020):** Officers contact ICE only "if a background check returns a possible hit from ICE regarding a wanted or detained person." Officers do not ask about immigration status during field encounters or traffic stops.

**Houston Chronicle Investigation Findings:**
- HPD officers called ICE **154 times in 2025** (some reports cite 107 calls since January 2025), a jump of **over 1,000%** from any of the previous five years
- The **vast majority** of those calls came from **traffic stops**
- In only **3 offense reports** were subjects suspected of serious crimes (domestic violence, aggravated robbery, narcotics)
- Just **12 officers** (out of 5,200+) were responsible for roughly **one-third** of all ICE calls
- Officers called ICE on people **reporting accidents or asking for help** at least **22 times**

**Specific policy violations documented:**
- In at least **two traffic stops**, officers handcuffed drivers, loaded them into patrol cars, and **personally drove them to ICE custody**
  - **Incident 1:** A man pulled over for expired registration; officers found an immigration violation in his records and drove him to the **Jersey Village police station** to hand him to ICE
  - **Incident 2:** A driver stopped for running a red light; officer found an administrative warrant, called ICE, and transported the driver to ICE custody
- Mayor Whitmire acknowledged this was **"a violation of Houston Police Department policy, and it will be corrected"**

**Political dispute:**
- Mayor Whitmire claims HPD was involved in only **74 ICE-related cases** in 2025, disputing reports linking Houston to 5,000+ deportations
- Council Member **Letitia Plummer** proposed revising HPD policy on when officers contact ICE
- Houston Police Officers' Union President **Doug Griffith** opposes changes, arguing officers cannot legally ignore warrants from any agency

Sources:
- [Houston Chronicle/Yahoo: HPD calls to ICE surged 1,000%](https://www.yahoo.com/news/articles/houston-police-calls-ice-surged-120021632.html)
- [ABC13: HPD relationship with ICE violates department policy](https://abc13.com/post/houston-police-department-relationship-ice-violates-policy-chronicle-report-says/18686247/)
- [Hoodline: Houston Cops Drove Pulled-Over Drivers Straight to ICE](https://hoodline.com/2026/03/houston-cops-drove-pulled-over-drivers-straight-to-ice-legal-heat-follows/)
- [Houston Landing: ICE arrest puts HPD protocols into question](https://www.houstonlanding.org/tell-the-truth-family-decries-hpds-role-in-houston-ice-arrest/)
- [Click2Houston: Mayor says HPD involved in 74 cases](https://www.click2houston.com/news/local/2026/01/08/houston-mayor-says-report-linking-city-to-over-5000-ice-deportations-untrue-says-hpd-involved-in-74-cases/)
- [Click2Houston: Council member proposes HPD policy change](https://www.click2houston.com/news/local/2025/11/21/houston-council-member-proposes-change-to-hpd-policy-on-ice-warrants-police-union-calls-it-impossible/)

## 4. Worksite Raids in Houston Area

ICE has conducted raids at **construction sites** across Texas, including the Houston area. As reported in early February 2026:
- Immigration raids at construction sites are driving up housing costs, hurting development, and delaying projects
- Companies in the supply chain report **sharp revenue declines**, with some filing for **bankruptcy**
- **Construction loans** in the region are down approximately **30%** over the past year
- Workers are avoiding job sites **even if they are citizens or have documents**, creating a broader chilling effect

Foreign-born noncitizens make up approximately **38.6%** of the Houston-area construction workforce (U.S. Census Bureau data).

## 6. Court and Probation Office Arrests

**Houston Immigration Court (Greenspoint Park), June 2025:**
- For **three consecutive days** (June 10-12, 2025), ICE officers arrested multiple people who showed up for hearings at the **Greenspoint Park Immigration Court** (16800 Greenspoint Park Dr.)
- Agents targeted individuals whose immigration cases had been **administratively closed**
- ICE focused on individuals in the U.S. for **less than two years**, regardless of pending asylum applications, lack of criminal history, or compliance with court appearances
- Advocates argued this undermines due process and discourages immigrants from attending hearings

More broadly across Texas in 2025-2026, ICE has arrested people at:
- Federal buildings in El Paso
- Immigration courts in San Antonio
- Probation offices in Dallas

The Trump administration **rescinded federal policies** that had limited arrests at sensitive locations (schools, churches, hospitals, courthouses).

## 7. Deportation Flights from Houston

**Project Homecoming (May 2025):**
- The first "self-deportation" flight departed from Houston, carrying **64 people** (38 to Honduras, 26 to Colombia)
- The program offered free flights and a **$1,000 stipend** to undocumented immigrants

**Avelo Airlines:**
- Houston-based Avelo Airlines signed a contract to fly ICE deportation flights, generating significant backlash
- Avelo subsequently announced it would **stop deportation flights** for DHS and cut commercial flights and jobs

**National scale:** Since January 20, 2025, the administration conducted at least **1,912 removal flights** to 79 countries and **7,362 domestic transfer flights**.

**Daily arrest rate:** Average ICE arrests in Texas jumped from **85/day** (final 18 months of Biden) to **176/day** (first six months under Trump).

## 8. Community Response, Protests, and Legal Aid

### Protests

**January 8-9, 2026:** Hundreds marched near the **Galleria** along **Post Oak Boulevard** and through the **Post Oak/Westheimer** intersection, protesting the January 7 fatal shooting of **Renee Nicole Good** (37) by an ICE agent in Minneapolis. Organized by the **Party for Socialism and Liberation** (Houston chapter). **FIEL Houston** organized a separate demonstration that Friday evening.

**Late January - February 2026: Student Walkouts**
- **January 30, 2026:** Students from **14 campuses** walked out to protest ICE operations
- **Houston Academy for International Studies:** 50+ students walked out
- **Cypress Falls High School:** Hundreds of students walked out
- Additional walkouts at **Conroe High School**, **Elkins High School**, and others
- Triggered by the deaths of Alex Pretti and Renee Good in separate federal agent shootings

**Government response to walkouts:**
- ICE Houston condemned "unlawful actions"
- TEA Commissioner **Mike Morath** threatened consequences: loss of attendance-based funding, teacher license investigations, and potential state takeover of districts
- Governor **Abbott** stated: "About time students like this were arrested"
- AG **Ken Paxton** opened an investigation into Austin ISD over walkouts

### Legal Aid Mobilization

- **Houston Immigrants' Rights Hotline:** 1-833-HOU-IMMI (1-833-468-4664), run by **HILSC** (Houston Immigration Legal Services Collaborative) with 5 nonprofit partners
- **ACLU of Texas Immigrants' Rights Hotline:** 1-833-HOU-IMMI
- **HILSC Immigrant Legal Services Fund:** Focused on detained individuals, providing representation for deportation cases

### Named Case: Curtis Wright
A **39-year-old legal permanent resident** from Canada, in the U.S. since 2001 (age 15), was detained on **November 6, 2025** at **George Bush Intercontinental Airport** returning from a business trip to Mexico. The trigger: a misdemeanor conviction for possessing a single Xanax tablet from 2003-2004 (when he was 17). His family argues federal law shields him from removal because 22+ years have passed since the offense, and ICE renewed his green card twice. As of late February 2026, he remained in detention. Fellow detainees nicknamed him "Professor" for teaching English classes.

## 9. Impact on Construction, Restaurant, and Service Industries

### Construction
- Foreign-born noncitizens = **38.6%** of Houston-area construction workforce
- Workers avoiding sites even with valid documents (chilling effect)
- Supply chain companies reporting **sharp revenue declines**; some filing **bankruptcy**
- Construction loans in the region **down ~30%** year-over-year
- Housing costs rising, development slowing, projects delayed

### Multifamily/Apartments (Arbor Realty Trust case study)
- **Arbor Realty Trust** CEO **Ivan Kaufman** stated ICE activity is directly impacting occupancy in Sun Belt markets, **primarily Houston** plus San Antonio, Dallas, Atlanta, and Florida
- Foreclosed properties averaging only **45% occupancy**
- Loan delinquencies hit **$570 million** at year-end; REO assets swelled to **$500 million** (nearly triple the $177 million at end of 2024)

### Survey Data (Rice University / Kinder Institute)
- **1 in 7 (15%)** of greater Houston residents say they personally know someone detained or deported by ICE
- Among **Hispanic Houstonians: 25%** know someone detained
- Among residents earning **under $35,000/year: 24%** know someone detained
- Among **Black residents: 9%**; **White residents: 8%**

# RIO GRANDE VALLEY (RGV)

## 1. Mariachi Family Detention (The Gamez-Cuellar Family)

On **February 25, 2026**, the Gamez-Cuellar family was detained by ICE in **McAllen, Texas** after being summoned to an ICE check-in and told to bring their sons. The family consists of:

- **Luis Antonio Gamez Martinez** and **Emma Guadalupe Cuellar Lopez** (parents)
- **Antonio** (18), **Caleb** (14), and **Joshua** (12) (sons)

Antonio and Caleb were members of **McAllen High School's Mariachi Oro**, an eight-time and current state championship program. The family entered the U.S. legally through the **CBP One app in May 2023**, passed a credible fear test, and had been compliant with all court dates and ICE check-ins for two years. Their final hearing was scheduled for **September 2026**.

Caleb, Joshua, and the parents were sent to the **South Texas Family Residential Center in Dilley**. Antonio (18) was transferred to the **El Valle Detention Facility in Raymondville**.

The case drew **bipartisan condemnation**. Rep. Monica De La Cruz (R-McAllen) said she was "heartbroken." Rep. Vicente Gonzalez pointed out the family had followed every legal process and was still detained.

## 4. Dilley Detention Facility (South Texas Family Residential Center)

**Capacity**: 2,400 (designed for women and children). Operated by **CoreCivic** under a 5-year contract; CoreCivic receives **$160 million annually**.

**Conditions (2026)**:
- **RAICES** filed a court document stating families raised concerns about inadequate medical care on **at least 700 occasions since August 2025**, including delays in follow-up for children's complaints.
- Mothers reported children were frequently ill, struggled to sleep on **metal bunks in crowded rooms**, and sometimes refused meals after reporting **worms or mold in food**.
- **ProPublica** (February 9, 2026) published an investigation including children's letters about their experiences.
- Reports of guards confiscating children's artwork and eavesdropping on attorney-client communications.

**Protests and Incidents**:
- **January 24, 2026**: Dozens of detainees staged a **mass protest** in the yard, yelling "libertad" and holding hand-drawn signs. The protest was triggered in part by the arrival of **Liam Conejo Ramos**, a 5-year-old in a blue bunny hat, on January 22.
- **February 1, 2026**: Approximately **100 people** held a vigil and protest outside the facility.
- **"Blue Butterfly Zones" controversy**: Social media rumors about secretive detention areas for teenage girls went viral. Fact-checkers traced the term to a 2015 LA Times article describing the facility's color-coded "neighborhoods" (red bird, yellow frog, blue butterfly) -- no verified documentation of the sinister claims emerged.

# EL PASO

## 1. Camp East Montana (Fort Bliss)

The nation's largest ICE detention facility, a **soft-sided tent structure** at Fort Bliss holding an average of **2,954 detainees** per day.

**Homicide of Geraldo Lunas Campos**:
- **January 3, 2026**: 55-year-old **Geraldo Lunas Campos**, a Cuban national, died in ICE custody at Camp East Montana.
- **January 21, 2026**: The **El Paso County Medical Examiner ruled the death a homicide**, caused by **asphyxia due to compression of the neck and torso**.
- DHS claimed Lunas Campos had attempted suicide.
- A **witness told the AP** that Lunas Campos was handcuffed as at least **five guards held him down** and one put an arm around his neck and squeezed until he was unconscious.
- The autopsy found **scattered superficial abrasions**, **hemorrhage in neck muscles and connective tissues**, and **petechial hemorrhaging on eyelids and neck skin**.
- Lunas Campos is one of **three detainees who have died** at Camp East Montana.

**Measles Outbreak**:
- **February 26, 2026**: **17 measles cases** reported in El Paso, including **13 at Camp East Montana**.
- **March 3, 2026**: Case count rose to **14 confirmed** at the camp, with **112 people in isolation**.
- The measles outbreak followed **two cases of tuberculosis** and **several COVID-19 cases** reported in **January 2026**.

**911 Calls / Conditions**:
- **March 6-7, 2026**: Reporting revealed 911 calls from inside the facility documenting **attempted suicides, fights**, medical distress, and widespread misery.
- Detainees describe loud and unsanitary quarters, inability to obtain medication, **significant weight loss from food shortages**, and fear of **private security guards known to use force**.

**Possible Closure**:
- **March 4, 2026**: Reports surfaced that ICE is **moving toward closing Camp East Montana**. A document distributed to ICE staff indicated the agency was drafting a letter to **terminate the facility's $1.2 billion contract** at an unspecified date.
- DHS is reviewing the contract in the wake of the homicide, measles outbreak, and ongoing conditions complaints.

## 4. El Paso County/City Cooperation with ICE

**County (Sheriff's Office)**:
- Under **Texas Senate Bill 8**, counties with populations over 100,000 must enter a **287(g) agreement** with ICE by January 2026.
- The **El Paso County Sheriff's Office (EPCSO)** chose the **detention-based model** (Jail Enforcement Model), which gives trained jail staff authority to question inmates about immigration status inside the jail. This is the least aggressive of the three options (vs. the Task Force Model allowing street-level enforcement).

**City (City Council -- opposition)**:
- **January 29, 2026**: El Paso City Council considered a **ban on ICE detention centers within city limits**.
- **February 2, 2026**: City Council **moved to reject** the proposed ICE mega-detention facility.
- The City Council voted on measures to **hold ICE accountable**, including requiring a report on all points of cooperation between the city and ICE.
- Separately, the **City of Socorro** moved to block the new detention center after community outcry.

**Mega Detention Center -- Clint**:
- **January 17, 2026**: DHS purchased industrial park warehouses in **Far East El Paso County near Clint** for **$123 million**.
- ICE plans an **8,500-capacity mega detention facility** on the property (three reinforced concrete warehouses of ~296,000 sq ft each).
- This is part of a **$38 billion national plan** for "immigrant mega-jails" (Axios, February 13, 2026).
- The facility would serve as a **deportation staging site** in a fast-moving pipeline.
