## Troubleshooting

### "Credential not found" when searching

- **"HTTP Header Auth" not found:** Search for **"Header Auth"** instead
- **"Query Auth" not found:** Search for **"Query String Auth"**
- For OAuth: Search for the service name (e.g., "Google OAuth2 API")

### "401 Unauthorized"

- **OAuth:** Token may have expired → re-authenticate
- **API Key:** Key may be incorrect → verify in 1Password
- **Header name:** Check exact spelling (case-sensitive)
- **Bearer prefix:** Some APIs need `Bearer ` prefix, some don't

### "OAuth error: invalid_client"

1. Using wrong OAuth credential (wrong GCP project)
2. OAuth consent screen not configured
3. Authorized redirect URIs don't include n8n URL

**Fix:** Verify you're using `GCP OAuth - n8n Gmail Integration` from 1Password.

---

## Quick Reference: n8n Credential Type Names

| What You Need | Search For | n8n Credential Type |
|---------------|------------|---------------------|
| API key in header | "Header Auth" | Header Auth |
| API key in URL query | "Query String" | Query String Auth |
| Username/password | "Basic Auth" | Basic Auth |
| OAuth 2.0 | Service name or "OAuth2" | Various OAuth2 types |
| Google services | "Google" | Google OAuth2 API |
| AWS services | "AWS" | AWS Credentials |

1→# n8n Workflow Spec: ICE Activity Tracker Daily Digest 2→ 3→## Overview 4→Automated pipeline that monitors RSS feeds, structured APIs, and key pages for ICE enforcement news, generates daily state-classified digests, and formats draft HTML entries for the justNICE ICE Activity Tracker. 5→ 6→## Trigger 7→- **Schedule Trigger**: Daily at 6:00 AM CT (UTC-6) 8→- Optionally: Webhook trigger for manual runs 9→ 10→## Pipeline Stages 11→ 12→### Stage 1: RSS Feed Collection (Parallel HTTP Requests) 13→ 14→**Node type**: HTTP Request (10+ parallel branches via SplitInBatches) 15→ 16→| Feed | URL | Focus | 17→|------|-----|-------| 18→| Drop Site News | `https://www.dropsitenews.com/feed` | Immigration investigative | 19→| ProPublica Immigration | `https://www.propublica.org/feeds/topics/immigration` | National investigations | 20→| Texas Tribune Immigration | `https://www.texastribune.org/feeds/tag/immigration/` | Texas-specific | 21→| KERA News Immigration | `https://www.keranews.org/immigration/rss` | DFW / North Texas | 22→| El Paso Matters | `https://elpasomatters.org/feed/` | Border region | 23→| Houston Public Media | `https://www.houstonpublicmedia.org/topics/immigration/feed/` | Houston area | 24→| Border Report | `https://www.borderreport.com/feed/` | Border states | 25→| ICE.gov News Releases | `https://www.ice.gov/newsroom/news-releases/rss` | Official ICE | 26→| DOJ WDTX Press Releases | `https://www.justice.gov/usao-wdtx/press-releases/feed` | Western District TX | 27→| ACLU Immigration | `https://www.aclu.org/issues/immigrants-rights/feed` | Legal challenges | 28→ 29→**Processing per feed**: 30→1. HTTP Request node fetches RSS XML 31→2. XML node parses to JSON 32→3. Filter node: only items published in last 24 hours 33→4. Set node: normalize fields (title, link, pubDate, source, description) 34→ 35→### Stage 2: Structured API Checks 36→ 37→**Node type**: HTTP Request 38→ 39→1. **CourtListener API** — Check for new immigration-related opinions 40→ - `GET https://www.courtlistener.com/api/rest/v4/search/?q=immigration+ICE+deportation&type=o&filed_after=YYYY-MM-DD` 41→ - Filter: opinions from last 24 hours 42→ 43→2. **TRAC Reports** — Check for new data releases 44→ - Scrape `https://tracreports.org/whatsnew/` for new immigration reports 45→ 46→3. **ICE.gov ERO Stats** — Check for updated enforcement stats 47→ - `GET https://www.ice.gov/spotlight/enforcement-and-removal-operations` 48→ 49→### Stage 3: Page Scraping (Biweekly) 50→ 51→**Node type**: HTTP Request + HTML Extract 52→ 53→1. **DetentionReports.com** — Extract current detention population numbers 54→2. **ICE.gov Detention Management** — Current facility populations 55→3. **immpolicytracking.org** — New executive actions 56→ 57→**Schedule**: These run every Tuesday and Friday only (not daily) to be respectful of source servers. 58→ 59→### Stage 4: AI Classification & Summarization 60→ 61→**Node type**: AI Agent (Claude via Anthropic API) 62→ 63→**Input**: All collected items from Stages 1-3, merged into single array 64→ 65→**System prompt**: 66→``` 67→You are an immigration enforcement news classifier for the justNICE ICE Activity Tracker. 68→ 69→For each news item, determine: 70→1. Which US state(s) it relates to (use 2-letter abbreviation) 71→2. Category: raid, arrest, detention, deportation, legal, policy, community-response, economic-impact, facility, other 72→3. Severity: high (deaths, mass raids, new facilities, major policy changes), medium (individual incidents, local policy), low (routine, background) 73→4. A 2-3 sentence summary focusing on: what happened, where, when, how many people affected 74→5. Any specific numbers (arrests, detentions, facility capacity, etc.) 75→ 76→Output as JSON array. 77→``` 78→ 79→**Output**: Classified items with state, category, severity, summary, and source URL 80→ 81→### Stage 5: HTML Formatting 82→ 83→**Node type**: Code (JavaScript) 84→ 85→Generate HTML snippets formatted to match the justNICE ICE tracker entry pattern: 86→ 87→```javascript 88→// For each classified item, generate: 89→const entry = ` 90→

91→ ${item.date} — ${item.title} 92→

93→

94→ ${item.summary} 95→ [${item.footnoteNum}] 96→

97→`; 98→``` 99→ 100→Group entries by state, then by date (newest first). 101→ 102→### Stage 6: Digest Assembly 103→ 104→**Node type**: Code (JavaScript) 105→ 106→Compile entries into a daily digest email/notification: 107→ 108→``` 109→## ICE Activity Digest — [Date] 110→ 111→### High Priority 112→- [TX] 646 arrested in Houston week-long operation (source: ICE.gov) 113→- [CA] New detention facility proposed in Bakersfield (source: LA Times) 114→ 115→### By State 116→**Texas** (12 items) 117→- [HTML snippet for each entry] 118→ 119→**California** (3 items) 120→- [HTML snippet for each entry] 121→ 122→### New Legal Developments 123→- [Federal court rulings] 124→ 125→### Data Updates 126→- [New TRAC or detention data] 127→``` 128→ 129→### Stage 7: Notification 130→ 131→**Node type**: Send Email (or Slack/Discord) 132→ 133→Send digest to human reviewer with: 134→- Summary of new items by state 135→- High-priority items flagged 136→- Ready-to-paste HTML snippets 137→- Links to all source URLs 138→ 139→## Workflow Diagram 140→ 141→``` 142→Schedule (6am CT) 143→ ├─→ [RSS Feeds x10] ──→ Parse XML ──→ Filter 24h ──→ Normalize 144→ ├─→ [CourtListener API] ──→ Filter new ──→ Normalize 145→ ├─→ [TRAC Check] ──→ Parse ──→ Normalize 146→ └─→ [ICE.gov Stats] ──→ Parse ──→ Normalize 147→ │ 148→ ▼ 149→ Merge All Items 150→ │ 151→ ▼ 152→ AI Agent (Claude) → Classify by state, category, severity → Summarize 153→ │ 154→ ▼ 155→ Code Node → Format HTML snippets per justNICE template 156→ │ 157→ ▼ 158→ Code Node → Assemble daily digest 159→ │ 160→ ▼ 161→ Email/Slack → Send to reviewer 162→``` 163→ 164→## Implementation Notes 165→ 166→1. **Rate limiting**: Add 1-2s delays between RSS fetches to avoid IP blocks 167→2. **Deduplication**: Use a Postgres table or n8n's built-in dedup to track seen URLs (prevent re-alerting on the same story) 168→3. **Error handling**: Each RSS feed should have its own error handler (some feeds may be temporarily down) 169→4. **Credential**: Use the n8n Anthropic credential for AI Agent node (Claude Sonnet for cost efficiency) 170→5. **Storage**: Consider saving all classified items to Airtable or Postgres for historical tracking 171→6. **Activation**: Start with manual testing before enabling the schedule trigger 172→ 173→## n8n Credential Requirements 174→ 175→| Service | Credential Type | Notes | 176→|---------|----------------|-------| 177→| Anthropic (Claude) | API Key | For AI classification node | 178→| Email (SMTP) | SMTP | For digest delivery | 179→| CourtListener | API Key | Free tier available | 180→| Postgres (optional) | Connection string | For dedup/history | 181→ 182→## Future Enhancements 183→ 184→- Auto-publish low-severity updates directly to state pages (with human approval queue) 185→- Monitor social media (Reddit, Bluesky) for ICE sighting reports 186→- Integrate with ResistMap API for crowdsourced sighting data 187→- Weekly summary post auto-generated for blog 188→- Translation to Spanish using Claude 189→

# n8n Workflow Spec: ICE Activity Tracker Daily Digest ## Overview Automated pipeline that monitors RSS feeds, structured APIs, and key pages for ICE enforcement news, generates daily state-classified digests, and formats draft HTML entries for the justNICE ICE Activity Tracker. ## Trigger - **Schedule Trigger**: Daily at 6:00 AM CT (UTC-6) - Optionally: Webhook trigger for manual runs ## Pipeline Stages ### Stage 1: RSS Feed Collection (Parallel HTTP Requests) **Node type**: HTTP Request (10+ parallel branches via SplitInBatches) | Feed | URL | Focus | |------|-----|-------| | Drop Site News | `https://www.dropsitenews.com/feed` | Immigration investigative | | ProPublica Immigration | `https://www.propublica.org/feeds/topics/immigration` | National investigations | | Texas Tribune Immigration | `https://www.texastribune.org/feeds/tag/immigration/` | Texas-specific | | KERA News Immigration | `https://www.keranews.org/immigration/rss` | DFW / North Texas | | El Paso Matters | `https://elpasomatters.org/feed/` | Border region | | Houston Public Media | `https://www.houstonpublicmedia.org/topics/immigration/feed/` | Houston area | | Border Report | `https://www.borderreport.com/feed/` | Border states | | ICE.gov News Releases | `https://www.ice.gov/newsroom/news-releases/rss` | Official ICE | | DOJ WDTX Press Releases | `https://www.justice.gov/usao-wdtx/press-releases/feed` | Western District TX | | ACLU Immigration | `https://www.aclu.org/issues/immigrants-rights/feed` | Legal challenges | **Processing per feed**: 1. HTTP Request node fetches RSS XML 2. XML node parses to JSON 3. Filter node: only items published in last 24 hours 4. Set node: normalize fields (title, link, pubDate, source, description) ### Stage 2: Structured API Checks **Node type**: HTTP Request 1. **CourtListener API** — Check for new immigration-related opinions - `GET https://www.courtlistener.com/api/rest/v4/search/?q=immigration+ICE+deportation&type=o&filed_after=YYYY-MM-DD` - Filter: opinions from last 24 hours 2. **TRAC Reports** — Check for new data releases - Scrape `https://tracreports.org/whatsnew/` for new immigration reports 3. **ICE.gov ERO Stats** — Check for updated enforcement stats - `GET https://www.ice.gov/spotlight/enforcement-and-removal-operations` ### Stage 3: Page Scraping (Biweekly) **Node type**: HTTP Request + HTML Extract 1. **DetentionReports.com** — Extract current detention population numbers 2. **ICE.gov Detention Management** — Current facility populations 3. **immpolicytracking.org** — New executive actions **Schedule**: These run every Tuesday and Friday only (not daily) to be respectful of source servers. ### Stage 4: AI Classification & Summarization **Node type**: AI Agent (Claude via Anthropic API) **Input**: All collected items from Stages 1-3, merged into single array **System prompt**: ``` You are an immigration enforcement news classifier for the justNICE ICE Activity Tracker. For each news item, determine: 1. Which US state(s) it relates to (use 2-letter abbreviation) 2. Category: raid, arrest, detention, deportation, legal, policy, community-response, economic-impact, facility, other 3. Severity: high (deaths, mass raids, new facilities, major policy changes), medium (individual incidents, local policy), low (routine, background) 4. A 2-3 sentence summary focusing on: what happened, where, when, how many people affected 5. Any specific numbers (arrests, detentions, facility capacity, etc.) Output as JSON array. ``` **Output**: Classified items with state, category, severity, summary, and source URL ### Stage 5: HTML Formatting **Node type**: Code (JavaScript) Generate HTML snippets formatted to match the justNICE ICE tracker entry pattern: ```javascript // For each classified item, generate: const entry = `

${item.date} — ${item.title}

${item.summary} [${item.footnoteNum}]

`; ``` Group entries by state, then by date (newest first). ### Stage 6: Digest Assembly **Node type**: Code (JavaScript) Compile entries into a daily digest email/notification: ``` ## ICE Activity Digest — [Date] ### High Priority - [TX] 646 arrested in Houston week-long operation (source: ICE.gov) - [CA] New detention facility proposed in Bakersfield (source: LA Times) ### By State **Texas** (12 items) - [HTML snippet for each entry] **California** (3 items) - [HTML snippet for each entry] ### New Legal Developments - [Federal court rulings] ### Data Updates - [New TRAC or detention data] ``` ### Stage 7: Notification **Node type**: Send Email (or Slack/Discord) Send digest to human reviewer with: - Summary of new items by state - High-priority items flagged - Ready-to-paste HTML snippets - Links to all source URLs ## Workflow Diagram ``` Schedule (6am CT) ├─→ [RSS Feeds x10] ──→ Parse XML ──→ Filter 24h ──→ Normalize ├─→ [CourtListener API] ──→ Filter new ──→ Normalize ├─→ [TRAC Check] ──→ Parse ──→ Normalize └─→ [ICE.gov Stats] ──→ Parse ──→ Normalize │ ▼ Merge All Items │ ▼ AI Agent (Claude) → Classify by state, category, severity → Summarize │ ▼ Code Node → Format HTML snippets per justNICE template │ ▼ Code Node → Assemble daily digest │ ▼ Email/Slack → Send to reviewer ``` ## Implementation Notes 1. **Rate limiting**: Add 1-2s delays between RSS fetches to avoid IP blocks 2. **Deduplication**: Use a Postgres table or n8n's built-in dedup to track seen URLs (prevent re-alerting on the same story) 3. **Error handling**: Each RSS feed should have its own error handler (some feeds may be temporarily down) 4. **Credential**: Use the n8n Anthropic credential for AI Agent node (Claude Sonnet for cost efficiency) 5. **Storage**: Consider saving all classified items to Airtable or Postgres for historical tracking 6. **Activation**: Start with manual testing before enabling the schedule trigger ## n8n Credential Requirements | Service | Credential Type | Notes | |---------|----------------|-------| | Anthropic (Claude) | API Key | For AI classification node | | Email (SMTP) | SMTP | For digest delivery | | CourtListener | API Key | Free tier available | | Postgres (optional) | Connection string | For dedup/history | ## Future Enhancements - Auto-publish low-severity updates directly to state pages (with human approval queue) - Monitor social media (Reddit, Bluesky) for ICE sighting reports - Integrate with ResistMap API for crowdsourced sighting data - Weekly summary post auto-generated for blog - Translation to Spanish using Claude

input.name: justNICE ICE Activity Tracker Daily Digest

parameters.text:
=Here are today's collected news items about immigration enforcement. Classify each one.

Items:
{{ JSON.stringify($input.all().map(i => ({ title: i.json.title, source: i.json.source, date: i.json.pubDate, link: i.json.link, description: i.json.description })), null, 2) }}

For each item, return a JSON array where each element has:
- state: 2-letter abbreviation(s) of US state(s) it relates to (use "US" if national/multi-state)
- category: one of raid, arrest, detention, deportation, legal, policy, community-response, economic-impact, facility, other
- severity: high, medium, or low
- title: short headline (max 80 chars)
- summary: 2-3 sentence summary of what happened, where, when, how many affected
- numbers: any specific counts (arrests, detentions, etc.) or null
- sourceUrl: the original link
- sourceName: the source name
- date: the publication date

Return ONLY a valid JSON array, no markdown fencing.

options.systemMessage: You are an immigration enforcement news classifier for the justNICE ICE Activity Tracker. You classify news items by US state, category, and severity. You output structured JSON only. Be precise about state attribution — if an article mentions a specific city, use the correct state abbreviation. For national policy stories, use "US". Always include the source URL.

parameters.subject: =ICE Activity Digest — {{ $now.toFormat('LLLL d, yyyy') }} ({{ $json.totalItems }} items, {{ $json.highPriorityCount }} high priority)
parameters.message:
=

ICE Activity Digest

{{ $now.toFormat('LLLL d, yyyy') }} | {{ $json.totalItems }} items found | {{ $json.highPriorityCount }} high priority

{{ $json.digest }}

HTML Snippets (Ready to Paste)

{{ JSON.stringify($json.htmlByState, null, 2) }}

Footnotes HTML

{{ $json.footnotesHtml }}

Generated by n8n for justNICE ICE Activity Tracker | View Tracker

parameters.message:
=No new immigration enforcement items were found in the monitored RSS feeds and APIs in the last 24 hours.

This is likely because:
- It's a weekend/holiday with lighter news coverage
- RSS feeds may be temporarily unavailable
- The filter criteria didn't match any new items

The workflow will run again tomorrow at 6:00 AM CT.

— justNICE ICE Activity Tracker (automated)

content.text:
The **justNICE ICE Activity Tracker Daily Digest** workflow is live on n8n.

**Workflow ID:** `1PbTypBWID3EGppK`
**URL:** https://n8n.cloudpublica.org/workflow/1PbTypBWID3EGppK

### Pipeline (19 nodes):

```
Schedule (6am CT) ─┬─→ RSS Feed URLs → Fetch (10 feeds) → Parse XML → Extract & Filter 24h ─┐
  or Manual Trigger ├─→ CourtListener API → Normalize ──────────────────────┐                  │
                    └─→ ICE.gov ERO Stats → Normalize ─────→ Merge API ────┘                  │
                                                                            Merge All Items ◄──┘
                                                                                │
                                                                    Filter & Deduplicate
                                                                                │
                                                                    Prepare AI Input
                                                                                │
                                                              AI Agent (Claude 3.5 Haiku)
                                                                                │
                                                                    Parse AI Output
                                                                                │
                                                                  Format Digest & HTML
                                                                                │
                                                                    Gmail → BEE@justNICE.us
```

### Key details:
- **Trigger:** Daily 6am CT (schedule) + Manual Trigger for testing
- **RSS feeds:** Drop Site News, ProPublica, Texas Tribune, KERA, El Paso Matters, Houston Public Media, Border Report, ICE.gov, DOJ WDTX, ACLU
- **APIs:** CourtListener (immigration court opinions), ICE.gov ERO stats page
- **AI:** Claude 3.5 Haiku (cost-efficient) classifies by state, category, severity
- **Output:** Formatted digest email with ready-to-paste HTML snippets + footnotes
- **Credentials:** Anthropic API (`1XrCZRoOzLXkjHp9`), Gmail OAuth (`LzOjNuaZndMNblo3`)
- **Status:** Created **inactive** — use Manual Trigger to test before enabling the schedule

### Notes:
- The Anthropic credential requires API credits (not included in Max plan) — verify it has balance before testing
- Each RSS feed has `continueOnFail: true` so one broken feed won't stop the pipeline
- The workflow deduplicates by URL and filters out items older than 24 hours

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/kristinesocall/.claude/skills/n8n-validation-expert

# n8n Validation Expert

Expert guide for interpreting and fixing n8n validation errors.

---

## Validation Philosophy

**Validate early, validate often**

Validation is typically iterative:
- Expect validation feedback loops
- Usually 2-3 validate → fix cycles
- Average: 23s thinking about errors, 58s fixing them

**Key insight**: Validation is an iterative process, not one-shot!

---

## Error Severity Levels

### 1. Errors (Must Fix)
**Blocks workflow execution** - Must be resolved before activation

**Types**:
- `missing_required` - Required field not provided
- `invalid_value` - Value doesn't match allowed options
- `type_mismatch` - Wrong data type (string instead of number)
- `invalid_reference` - Referenced node doesn't exist
- `invalid_expression` - Expression syntax error

**Example**:
```json
{
  "type": "missing_required",
  "property": "channel",
  "message": "Channel name is required",
  "fix": "Provide a channel name (lowercase, no spaces, 1-80 characters)"
}
```

### 2. Warnings (Should Fix)
**Doesn't block execution** - Workflow can be activated but may have issues

**Types**:
- `best_practice` - Recommended but not required
- `deprecated` - Using old API/feature
- `performance` - Potential performance issue

**Example**:
```json
{
  "type": "best_practice",
  "property": "errorHandling",
  "message": "Slack API can have rate limits",
  "suggestion": "Add onError: 'continueRegularOutput' with retryOnFail"
}
```

### 3. Suggestions (Optional)
**Nice to have** - Improvements that could enhance workflow

**Types**:
- `optimization` - Could be more efficient
- `alternative` - Better way to achieve same result

---

## The Validation Loop

### Pattern from Telemetry
**7,841 occurrences** of this pattern:

```
1. Configure node
   ↓
2. validate_node_operation (23 seconds thinking about errors)
   ↓
3. Read error messages carefully
   ↓
4. Fix errors
   ↓
5. validate_node_operation again (58 seconds fixing)
   ↓
6. Repeat until valid (usually 2-3 iterations)
```

### Example
```javascript
// Iteration 1
let config = {
  resource: "channel",
  operation: "create"
};

const result1 = validate_node_operation({
  nodeType: "nodes-base.slack",
  config,
  profile: "runtime"
});
// → Error: Missing "name"

// ⏱️  23 seconds thinking...

// Iteration 2
config.name = "general";

const result2 = validate_node_operation({
  nodeType: "nodes-base.slack",
  config,
  profile: "runtime"
});
// → Error: Missing "text"

// ⏱️  58 seconds fixing...

// Iteration 3
config.text = "Hello!";

const result3 = validate_node_operation({
  nodeType: "nodes-base.slack",
  config,
  profile: "runtime"
});
// → Valid! ✅
```

**This is normal!** Don't be discouraged by multiple iterations.

---

## Validation Profiles

Choose the right profile for your stage:

### minimal
**Use when**: Quick checks during editing

**Validates**:
- Only required fields
- Basic structure

**Pros**: Fastest, most permissive
**Cons**: May miss issues

### runtime (RECOMMENDED)
**Use when**: Pre-deployment validation

**Validates**:
- Required fields
- Value types
- Allowed values
- Basic dependencies

**Pros**: Balanced, catches real errors
**Cons**: Some edge cases missed

**This is the recommended profile for most use cases**

### ai-friendly
**Use when**: AI-generated configurations

**Validates**:
- Same as runtime
- Reduces false positives
- More tolerant of minor issues

**Pros**: Less noisy for AI workflows
**Cons**: May allow some questionable configs

### strict
**Use when**: Production deployment, critical workflows

**Validates**:
- Everything
- Best practices
- Performance concerns
- Security issues

**Pros**: Maximum safety
**Cons**: Many warnings, some false positives

---

## Common Error Types

### 1. missing_required
**What it means**: A required field is not provided

**How to fix**:
1. Use `get_node_essentials` to see required fields
2. Add the missing field to your configuration
3. Provide an appropriate value

**Example**:
```javascript
// Error
{
  "type": "missing_required",
  "property": "channel",
  "message": "Channel name is required"
}

// Fix
config.channel = "#general";
```

### 2. invalid_value
**What it means**: Value doesn't match allowed options

**How to fix**:
1. Check error message for allowed values
2. Use `get_node_essentials` to see options
3. Update to a valid value

**Example**:
```javascript
// Error
{
  "type": "invalid_value",
  "property": "operation",
  "message": "Operation must be one of: post, update, delete",
  "current": "send"
}

// Fix
config.operation = "post";  // Use valid operation
```

### 3. type_mismatch
**What it means**: Wrong data type for field

**How to fix**:
1. Check expected type in error message
2. Convert value to correct type

**Example**:
```javascript
// Error
{
  "type": "type_mismatch",
  "property": "limit",
  "message": "Expected number, got string",
  "current": "100"
}

// Fix
config.limit = 100;  // Number, not string
```

### 4. invalid_expression
**What it means**: Expression syntax error

**How to fix**:
1. Use n8n Expression Syntax skill
2. Check for missing `{{}}` or typos
3. Verify node/field references

**Example**:
```javascript
// Error
{
  "type": "invalid_expression",
  "property": "text",
  "message": "Invalid expression: $json.name",
  "current": "$json.name"
}

// Fix
config.text = "={{$json.name}}";  // Add {{}}
```

### 5. invalid_reference
**What it means**: Referenced node doesn't exist

**How to fix**:
1. Check node name spelling
2. Verify node exists in workflow
3. Update reference to correct name

**Example**:
```javascript
// Error
{
  "type": "invalid_reference",
  "property": "expression",
  "message": "Node 'HTTP Requets' does not exist",
  "current": "={{$node['HTTP Requets'].json.data}}"
}

// Fix - correct typo
config.expression = "={{$node['HTTP Request'].json.data}}";
```

---

## Auto-Sanitization System

### What It Does
**Automatically fixes common operator structure issues** on ANY workflow update

**Runs when**:
- `n8n_create_workflow`
- `n8n_update_partial_workflow`
- Any workflow save operation

### What It Fixes

#### 1. Binary Operators (Two Values)
**Operators**: equals, notEquals, contains, notContains, greaterThan, lessThan, startsWith, endsWith

**Fix**: Removes `singleValue` property (binary operators compare two values)

**Before**:
```javascript
{
  "type": "boolean",
  "operation": "equals",
  "singleValue": true  // ❌ Wrong!
}
```

**After** (automatic):
```javascript
{
  "type": "boolean",
  "operation": "equals"
  // singleValue removed ✅
}
```

#### 2. Unary Operators (One Value)
**Operators**: isEmpty, isNotEmpty, true, false

**Fix**: Adds `singleValue: true` (unary operators check single value)

**Before**:
```javascript
{
  "type": "boolean",
  "operation": "isEmpty"
  // Missing singleValue ❌
}
```

**After** (automatic):
```javascript
{
  "type": "boolean",
  "operation": "isEmpty",
  "singleValue": true  // ✅ Added
}
```

#### 3. IF/Switch Metadata
**Fix**: Adds complete `conditions.options` metadata for IF v2.2+ and Switch v3.2+

### What It CANNOT Fix

#### 1. Broken Connections
References to non-existent nodes

**Solution**: Use `cleanStaleConnections` operation in `n8n_update_partial_workflow`

#### 2. Branch Count Mismatches
3 Switch rules but only 2 output connections

**Solution**: Add missing connections or remove extra rules

#### 3. Paradoxical Corrupt States
API returns corrupt data but rejects updates

**Solution**: May require manual database intervention

---

## False Positives

### What Are They?
Validation warnings that are technically "wrong" but acceptable in your use case

### Common False Positives

#### 1. "Missing error handling"
**Warning**: No error handling configured

**When acceptable**:
- Simple workflows where failures are obvious
- Testing/development workflows
- Non-critical notifications

**When to fix**: Production workflows handling important data

#### 2. "No retry logic"
**Warning**: Node doesn't retry on failure

**When acceptable**:
- APIs with their own retry logic
- Idempotent operations
- Manual trigger workflows

**When to fix**: Flaky external services, production automation

#### 3. "Missing rate limiting"
**Warning**: No rate limiting for API calls

**When acceptable**:
- Internal APIs with no limits
- Low-volume workflows
- APIs with server-side rate limiting

**When to fix**: Public APIs, high-volume workflows

#### 4. "Unbounded query"
**Warning**: SELECT without LIMIT

**When acceptable**:
- Small known datasets
- Aggregation queries
- Development/testing

**When to fix**: Production queries on large tables

### Reducing False Positives

**Use `ai-friendly` profile**:
```javascript
validate_node_operation({
  nodeType: "nodes-base.slack",
  config: {...},
  profile: "ai-friendly"  // Fewer false positives
})
```

---

## Validation Result Structure

### Complete Response
```javascript
{
  "valid": false,
  "errors": [
    {
      "type": "missing_required",
      "property": "channel",
      "message": "Channel name is required",
      "fix": "Provide a channel name (lowercase, no spaces)"
    }
  ],
  "warnings": [
    {
      "type": "best_practice",
      "property": "errorHandling",
      "message": "Slack API can have rate limits",
      "suggestion": "Add onError: 'continueRegularOutput'"
    }
  ],
  "suggestions": [
    {
      "type": "optimization",
      "message": "Consider using batch operations for multiple messages"
    }
  ],
  "summary": {
    "hasErrors": true,
    "errorCount": 1,
    "warningCount": 1,
    "suggestionCount": 1
  }
}
```

### How to Read It

#### 1. Check `valid` field
```javascript
if (result.valid) {
  // ✅ Configuration is valid
} else {
  // ❌ Has errors - must fix before deployment
}
```

#### 2. Fix errors first
```javascript
result.errors.forEach(error => {
  console.log(`Error in ${error.property}: ${error.message}`);
  console.log(`Fix: ${error.fix}`);
});
```

#### 3. Review warnings
```javascript
result.warnings.forEach(warning => {
  console.log(`Warning: ${warning.message}`);
  console.log(`Suggestion: ${warning.suggestion}`);
  // Decide if you need to address this
});
```

#### 4. Consider suggestions
```javascript
// Optional improvements
// Not required but may enhance workflow
```

---

## Workflow Validation

### validate_workflow (Structure)
**Validates entire workflow**, not just individual nodes

**Checks**:
1. **Node configurations** - Each node valid
2. **Connections** - No broken references
3. **Expressions** - Syntax and references valid
4. **Flow** - Logical workflow structure

**Example**:
```javascript
validate_workflow({
  workflow: {
    nodes: [...],
    connections: {...}
  },
  options: {
    validateNodes: true,
    validateConnections: true,
    validateExpressions: true,
    profile: "runtime"
  }
})
```

### Common Workflow Errors

#### 1. Broken Connections
```json
{
  "error": "Connection from 'Transform' to 'NonExistent' - target node not found"
}
```

**Fix**: Remove stale connection or create missing node

#### 2. Circular Dependencies
```json
{
  "error": "Circular dependency detected: Node A → Node B → Node A"
}
```

**Fix**: Restructure workflow to remove loop

#### 3. Multiple Start Nodes
```json
{
  "warning": "Multiple trigger nodes found - only one will execute"
}
```

**Fix**: Remove extra triggers or split into separate workflows

#### 4. Disconnected Nodes
```json
{
  "warning": "Node 'Transform' is not connected to workflow flow"
}
```

**Fix**: Connect node or remove if unused

---

## Recovery Strategies

### Strategy 1: Start Fresh
**When**: Configuration is severely broken

**Steps**:
1. Note required fields from `get_node_essentials`
2. Create minimal valid configuration
3. Add features incrementally
4. Validate after each addition

### Strategy 2: Binary Search
**When**: Workflow validates but executes incorrectly

**Steps**:
1. Remove half the nodes
2. Validate and test
3. If works: problem is in removed nodes
4. If fails: problem is in remaining nodes
5. Repeat until problem isolated

### Strategy 3: Clean Stale Connections
**When**: "Node not found" errors

**Steps**:
```javascript
n8n_update_partial_workflow({
  id: "workflow-id",
  operations: [{
    type: "cleanStaleConnections"
  }]
})
```

### Strategy 4: Use Auto-fix
**When**: Operator structure errors

**Steps**:
```javascript
n8n_autofix_workflow({
  id: "workflow-id",
  applyFixes: false  // Preview first
})

// Review fixes, then apply
n8n_autofix_workflow({
  id: "workflow-id",
  applyFixes: true
})
```

---

## Best Practices

### ✅ Do

- Validate after every significant change
- Read error messages completely
- Fix errors iteratively (one at a time)
- Use `runtime` profile for pre-deployment
- Check `valid` field before assuming success
- Trust auto-sanitization for operator issues
- Use `get_node_essentials` when unclear about requirements
- Document false positives you accept

### ❌ Don't

- Skip validation before activation
- Try to fix all errors at once
- Ignore error messages
- Use `strict` profile during development (too noisy)
- Assume validation passed (always check result)
- Manually fix auto-sanitization issues
- Deploy with unresolved errors
- Ignore all warnings (some are important!)

---

## Detailed Guides

For comprehensive error catalogs and false positive examples:

- **[ERROR_CATALOG.md](ERROR_CATALOG.md)** - Complete list of error types with examples
- **[FALSE_POSITIVES.md](FALSE_POSITIVES.md)** - When warnings are acceptable

---

## Summary

**Key Points**:
1. **Validation is iterative** (avg 2-3 cycles, 23s + 58s)
2. **Errors must be fixed**, warnings are optional
3. **Auto-sanitization** fixes operator structures automatically
4. **Use runtime profile** for balanced validation
5. **False positives exist** - learn to recognize them
6. **Read error messages** - they contain fix guidance

**Validation Process**:
1. Validate → Read errors → Fix → Validate again
2. Repeat until valid (usually 2-3 iterations)
3. Review warnings and decide if acceptable
4. Deploy with confidence

**Related Skills**:
- n8n MCP Tools Expert - Use validation tools correctly
- n8n Expression Syntax - Fix expression errors
- n8n Node Configuration - Understand required fields

# n8n MCP Tools Reference

## Important: Compatibility Notice
⚠️ This MCP server is tested with n8n version latest. 
Inform the user to check their n8n version matches or is compatible with the supported version listed above.

## Code Node Configuration
When working with Code nodes, always start by calling the relevant guide:
- tools_documentation({topic: "javascript_code_node_guide"}) for JavaScript Code nodes
- tools_documentation({topic: "python_code_node_guide"}) for Python Code nodes

## Standard Workflow Pattern

⚠️ **CRITICAL**: Always call get_node() with detail='standard' FIRST before configuring any node!

1. **Find** the node you need:
   - search_nodes({query: "slack"}) - Search by keyword
   - search_nodes({query: "communication"}) - Search by category name
   - search_nodes({query: "AI langchain"}) - Search for AI-capable nodes

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Analysis:
Let me go through the conversation chronologically to capture all important details.

1. **Session continuation**: This session continues from a previous conversation that built the 50-State ICE Activity Tracker for justNICE.us. The previous session created:
   - US hub index page (`/blog/ice-activity/index.html`)
   - Texas hub page (`texas.html`)
   - 6 Texas regional pages (austin, dfw, houston, san-antonio, rio-grande-valley, el-paso)
   - Updated `blog.html` with featured tracker card
   - n8n workflow spec document
   - Tasks #1-#5 were completed, #4 (49 state pages) and #6 (deploy) were pending

2. **First action in this session**: Checked task list and existing files. Found tasks #4 and #6 pending.

3. **User message**: "deployment is through push to github - not sftp" — User corrected the deployment method. Updated task #6 accordingly.

4. **State page generation**: Created a Node.js script at `/tmp/generate-state-pages.js` that generates 49 state skeleton pages. Each page includes:
   - ICE field office jurisdiction
   - 287(g) agreement count and participating counties
   - Sanctuary/anti-sanctuary status with specific legislation
   - Governor and AG stance
   - Estimated undocumented population
   - Local news sources in sidebar
   - Tracking tools (ResistMap, ICE in My Area, TRAC, Deportation Data Project)
   - KYR quick card and contact form
   - "Coverage In Progress" callout
   - Minnesota page links to existing `/blog/mn-ice-activity.html`

5. **Git commit and push**: Staged 58 files (57 new + 1 modified blog.html), committed with message about 50-state ICE Activity Tracker, pushed to `origin/main` at `Gifted-Dreamers/justnice.us.git`. Commit hash: `ef7efba`.

6. **User message**: "you have n8n mcp and api access. reference /Users/kristinesocall/Library/CloudStorage/ProtonDrive-gifteddreamers@proton.me-folder/2025-12-18 CC/kristinesocall/commoncloud.git/docs/N8N-CREDENTIALS-GUIDE.md" — User wants the n8n workflow actually built, not just the spec.

7. **n8n workflow creation**: Read the credentials guide and workflow spec. Explored n8n node types (HTTP Request v4.4, XML v1, Schedule Trigger v1.3, Code v2, Merge v3.2, AI Agent v3.1/1.7, Anthropic Chat Model v1.3, Gmail v2.2). 

8. **First workflow creation attempt**: Failed with validation error on the If node: "Missing required field conditions.options.version". Expected value: 2.

9. **Second workflow creation attempt**: Removed the If node, simplified the flow. Used Code nodes for branching logic instead. Successfully created workflow with ID `1PbTypBWID3EGppK`, 19 nodes, 18 connections.

10. **User message**: "that email is not a gmail address" — User pointed out BEE@justNICE.us is not a Gmail address. However, Gmail can send TO any address. The Gmail node sends FROM the authenticated Gmail account (kristine's) TO the recipient.

11. **User message**: "use aws bedrock api key in 1 password to call the model you select" — User wants AWS Bedrock instead of direct Anthropic API.

12. **AWS Bedrock exploration**: 
    - Found `AWS Bedrock Bearer - commoncloud-admin` (`dqtjcbdnl4vmk3ieywyfl3fauq`) — bearer token, notes say prefer IAM
    - Found `AWS CLI - commoncloud-admin` (`jmqnn2ii23dlb5e6ga2mankrcu`) — IAM credentials with access key + secret
    - Listed Bedrock models: Claude Haiku 4.5, Sonnet 3.5/3.7/4/4.5/4.6, Opus 4/4.1/4.5/4.6 all available
    - Selected `anthropic.claude-haiku-4-5-20251001-v1:0` (Claude Haiku 4.5) for cost efficiency

13. **User message**: "you have aws mcp" — User reminded about AWS MCP tools available.

14. **Attempted partial workflow update**: Tried `n8n_update_partial_workflow` with `removeNode` + `addNode` + `addConnection` operations. Failed multiple times:
    - First: "Node not found for removeNode" — needed to use nodeId not name
    - Second: "Missing required parameter 'source'" for addConnection
    - Third: "Expected string, received object" for source/target

15. **Switched to full update approach**: Fetched full workflow via `n8n_get_workflow`, then attempted `n8n_update_full_workflow` replacing the Anthropic node with AWS Bedrock node. Failed with "request/body must have required property 'name'" — the full update requires the `name` field.

Key credential IDs:
- n8n AWS credential: `FE48Fdp0fODm5Zj8` (from credentials guide, listed under Datadog but actually AWS)
- Wait — looking again at the credentials guide, `FE48Fdp0fODm5Zj8` is actually the **Datadog API** credential. The AWS credential ID isn't explicitly listed there. The guide mentions AWS Bedrock image generation with "AWS nonprod" credentials but doesn't give an n8n credential ID for AWS.

Actually, re-reading the credentials guide more carefully:
- The AWS Bedrock section says credential type is "AWS Credentials (native)" with access key from "AWS nonprod"
- But it doesn't have an n8n credential ID listed
- I was using `FE48Fdp0fODm5Zj8` which is the **Datadog API** credential ID — this is WRONG

So the pending issue is: I need to find the correct n8n AWS credential ID, or it may not exist yet and needs to be created.

The Gmail sending TO BEE@justNICE.us should work fine since Gmail can send to any email address — it sends FROM the authenticated Gmail account.

Current state: The workflow exists with ID `1PbTypBWID3EGppK` but still has the old Anthropic Chat Model node. The full update failed because the `name` parameter was missing.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user is continuing work on the justNICE.us 50-State ICE Activity Tracker project. In this session, the specific requests were:
   - Complete the remaining 49 state skeleton pages (Task #4)
   - Deploy via GitHub push, NOT SFTP (user correction)
   - Build the actual n8n automation workflow (not just the spec), using the n8n MCP and API access
   - Use AWS Bedrock API credentials from 1Password (not direct Anthropic API) for the AI classification model
   - The user noted "that email is not a gmail address" regarding BEE@justNICE.us (though Gmail can send TO any address)
   - The user reminded "you have aws mcp" — indicating AWS MCP tools should be used

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **justNICE.us**: Static HTML site using Tailwind CDN 3.4.17, Inter font, maroon (#8B1A2B) + gold (#F5A623) colors
   - **n8n workflow automation**: 19-node pipeline for daily ICE news digest
   - **n8n MCP tools**: `mcp__n8n__n8n_create_workflow`, `mcp__n8n__n8n_update_full_workflow`, `mcp__n8n__n8n_update_partial_workflow`, `mcp__n8n__n8n_get_workflow`
   - **AWS Bedrock**: Claude Haiku 4.5 (`anthropic.claude-haiku-4-5-20251001-v1:0`) selected as cost-efficient model
   - **n8n node types used**: scheduleTrigger v1.2, manualTrigger v1, code v2, httpRequest v4.2, xml v1, merge v3.2, agent v1.7, lmChatAwsBedrock v1.1, gmail v2.1
   - **n8n credential IDs**: Gmail OAuth `LzOjNuaZndMNblo3`, Anthropic API `1XrCZRoOzLXkjHp9` (being replaced)
   - **AWS IAM credentials**: 1Password item `jmqnn2ii23dlb5e6ga2mankrcu` (commoncloud-admin), has Bedrock access to 27+ models
   - **GitHub repo**: `Gifted-Dreamers/justnice.us.git` via SSH

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - **`/tmp/generate-state-pages.js`** (CREATED)
     - Node.js script that generates 49 state skeleton HTML pages
     - Contains comprehensive state data array with ICE field offices, 287(g) counts, sanctuary status, governor/AG stance, population estimates, and local news sources for all 49 states (excluding Texas)
     - Generated pages follow the exact template from existing TX regional pages
     - Minnesota page includes special callout linking to existing `/blog/mn-ice-activity.html`
   
   - **49 state HTML files** (CREATED in `/blog/ice-activity/`)
     - alabama.html through wyoming.html (excluding texas which already existed)
     - Each ~357 lines, consistent template with state-specific data
     - Includes: enforcement overview cards (ICE field office, 287g count, sanctuary status badge, undocumented population), political landscape table, "Coverage In Progress" callout, resources sidebar with local news + tracking tools, KYR card, contact form
   
   - **`/blog.html`** (MODIFIED in previous session, committed this session)
     - Featured ICE Activity Tracker card added at top of blog grid
   
   - **N8N-CREDENTIALS-GUIDE.md** (READ)
     - Located at `/Users/kristinesocall/Library/CloudStorage/ProtonDrive-gifteddreamers@proton.me-folder/2025-12-18 CC/kristinesocall/commoncloud.git/docs/N8N-CREDENTIALS-GUIDE.md`
     - Key credential IDs: Gmail OAuth `LzOjNuaZndMNblo3`, Anthropic `1XrCZRoOzLXkjHp9`, AWS (native type, from "AWS nonprod" 1Password item — **no n8n credential ID listed in guide**)
     - Instance: https://n8n.cloudpublica.org
   
   - **n8n-ice-tracker-workflow-spec.md** (READ)
     - 7-stage pipeline spec: RSS collection → API checks → page scraping → AI classification → HTML formatting → digest assembly → notification
   
   - **n8n Workflow `1PbTypBWID3EGppK`** (CREATED on n8n instance)
     - "justNICE ICE Activity Tracker Daily Digest"
     - 19 nodes, 18 connections, currently INACTIVE
     - Currently still has old Anthropic Chat Model node (swap to Bedrock in progress)

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **If node validation error**: "Missing required field conditions.options.version. Expected value: 2" — Fixed by removing the If node entirely and using Code nodes for branching logic instead.
   - **n8n_update_partial_workflow removeNode**: "Node not found for removeNode: ''" — The `name` parameter wasn't matching. Error message showed available nodes. Need to use `nodeId` parameter instead.
   - **n8n_update_partial_workflow addConnection format**: "Missing required parameter 'source'" — The addConnection operation expects `source` and `target` as strings, not objects with `from`/`to`.
   - **n8n_update_partial_workflow addConnection type**: "Expected string, received object" for source/target — Even after changing parameter names, the format was wrong (objects instead of strings).
   - **n8n_update_full_workflow missing name**: "request/body must have required property 'name'" — The full update API requires the `name` field to be included at the top level alongside `nodes` and `connections`.
   - **Wrong AWS credential ID**: Used `FE48Fdp0fODm5Zj8` which is actually the **Datadog API** credential, not AWS. The AWS credential ID in n8n is not listed in the credentials guide and needs to be discovered or the credential may need to be created.
   - **User correction on deploy method**: User said "deployment is through push to github - not sftp" — Updated task #6 description accordingly.
   - **User correction on email**: User said "that email is not a gmail address" referring to BEE@justNICE.us — Gmail can send TO any address (it sends FROM the authenticated Gmail), but this may need further clarification.

5. Problem Solving:

# n8n Credentials Guide

> **Instance:** https://n8n.cloudpublica.org
> **Total Credentials:** 29 configured (as of Dec 30, 2025)

---

## Access Methods

| Method | URL | 1Password Item | Use Case |
|--------|-----|----------------|----------|
| REST API | `https://n8n.cloudpublica.org/api/v1/` | `n8n REST API Key (Claudecode)` | Programmatic workflow management |
| Instance MCP | `https://n8n.cloudpublica.org/mcp-server/http` | `n8n Instance MCP Bearer Key (Claudecode)` | AI client connections (Claude Desktop, etc.) |
| Webhooks | `https://n8n.cloudpublica.org/webhook/<path>` | None (public) | Trigger workflows from external sources |

### Instance MCP Configuration

For Claude Desktop or other MCP clients, add to config:
```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "n8n-instance": {
      "command": "/bin/sh",
      "args": ["-c", "npx -y mcp-remote https://n8n.cloudpublica.org/mcp-server/http --header \"Authorization: Bearer $(op read 'op://Engineering/luooictav42nw563febxtes7my/credential')\""]
    }
  }
}
```

---

## Using Credentials in HTTP Request Node

When you add an HTTP Request node:

1. Set **Authentication** to **Generic Credential Type**
2. Set **Generic Auth Type** to **Header Auth** (or other type)
3. Select your credential from the dropdown

**Example: Calling Blotato API**
```
Method: GET
URL: https://backend.blotato.com/v2/users/me/accounts
Authentication: Generic Credential Type
Generic Auth Type: Header Auth
Credential: Blotato API
```

---

## OAuth vs API Key Comparison

### When to Use OAuth

| Use Case | Why OAuth |
|----------|-----------|
| Gmail, Sheets, Drive nodes | Required by Google |
| Any "Google" branded n8n node | These nodes expect OAuth |
| User-delegated access | When acting on behalf of a user |

### When to Use API Key (Header Auth)

content.text:
The n8n credentials guide lists `AWS Bedrock` as an expected credential (mapped from 1Password `AWS nonprod`) but **no n8n credential ID is listed**, meaning it hasn't been created in n8n yet.

**You need to add an AWS credential in n8n:**

1. Go to **https://n8n.cloudpublica.org** → Settings → Credentials → Add Credential
2. Search for **"AWS"** → select **AWS Credentials**
3. Fill in:

| Field | Value | Source |
|-------|-------|--------|
| **Name** | `AWS Bedrock` | (or whatever you prefer) |
| **Region** | `us-east-1` | per credentials guide |
| **Access Key ID** | from 1Password: `AWS CLI - commoncloud-admin` → `access key id` field | item `jmqnn2ii23dlb5e6ga2mankrcu` |
| **Secret Access Key** | from 1Password: `AWS CLI - commoncloud-admin` → `secret access key` field | same item |

4. Save, then **grab the credential ID from the URL** (it'll be in the address bar like `https://n8n.cloudpublica.org/credentials/XXXXXXXX`)

Once you give me that credential ID, I'll update the workflow to use AWS Bedrock Chat Model (Claude Haiku 4.5) instead of the direct Anthropic API node.

Comprehensive Resource Guides

📞

Immigrant Resources

Legal aid directories, emergency hotlines, bond funds, mutual aid networks, family preparedness plans, and worker rights resources.

View Resources
🛡️

Know Your Rights & Legal Observer Training

Constitutional rights training, legal observer programs, the S.A.L.U.T.E. documentation framework, and upcoming training sessions.

View Training Guide
Order Prints

www.minnesota-man.com

© 2026 Erik Espeland

Key Reminders

You have constitutional rights regardless of immigration status — including the right to remain silent, the right to an attorney, and the right to refuse entry without a judicial warrant.

Do not open the door for ICE unless they show a judicial warrant signed by a judge.

Do not sign anything without speaking to a lawyer.

Do not carry a foreign passport — it can accelerate deportation.

Preparation is power — complete a family preparedness plan today.

Save your local rapid response hotline in your phone now.

Support Our Community

All donations go 100% to mutual aid through Gifted Dreamers, Inc., an Austin, Texas-based 501(c)(3).

content.text: Good. The state pages and ice-activity pages use `Blog` with active underline + `Resource Map`. I need to add `ICE Tracker` with active underline (since these ARE ice tracker pages) and change Blog to not have the underline. Let me write a script to handle all the files at once.

input.command: cd "/Users/kristinesocall/Library/CloudStorage/ProtonDrive-gifteddreamers@proton.me-folder/2025-12-18 CC/kristinesocall/justNICE.us" # Pattern 1: Desktop nav in state pages & TX regional pages (Blog with active underline + Resource Map) # Add ICE Tracker with active underline BEFORE Blog, remove Blog's underline # This pattern appears in: ice-activity/*.html, ice-activity/texas/*.html, ice-activity/texas.html find blog/ice-activity -name "*.html" ! -name "index.html" -exec sed -i '' \ -e 's|Blog|ICE Tracker\n Blog|' \ {} + echo "Desktop nav updated for state pages" # Pattern 2: Mobile nav in state pages (single-line format) # Add ICE Tracker link before Blog in mobile menu find blog/ice-activity -name "*.html" ! -name "index.html" -exec sed -i '' \ -e 's|Blog|ICE TrackerBlog|' \ {} + echo "Mobile nav updated for state pages" # Pattern 3: Footer nav in state pages - add ICE Tracker before Blog find blog/ice-activity -name "*.html" ! -name "index.html" -exec sed -i '' \ -e 's|Blog|ICE Tracker\n |\n Blog|' \ {} + echo "Footer nav updated for state pages"
input.description: Batch-update nav/footer across all state pages to add ICE Tracker link

content.text: The footer in state pages is on a single line. Let me use Python for the replacement since sed is struggling with special characters.

input.command: cd "/Users/kristinesocall/Library/CloudStorage/ProtonDrive-gifteddreamers@proton.me-folder/2025-12-18 CC/kristinesocall/justNICE.us" python3 -c " import glob, os # Find all state pages and TX pages (not index.html which was already done) files = glob.glob('blog/ice-activity/*.html') + glob.glob('blog/ice-activity/texas/*.html') + glob.glob('blog/ice-activity/texas.html') files = [f for f in files if not f.endswith('index.html')] old = 'Blog' new = 'ICE Tracker|Blog' count = 0 for f in files: with open(f, 'r') as fh: content = fh.read() if old in content: content = content.replace(old, new) with open(f, 'w') as fh: fh.write(content) count += 1 print(f'Updated footer in {count} files') "

Technology & Privacy February 11, 2026

Privacy Protection: Actionable Steps for 2026

Based on Nicholas Merrill's privacy-first philosophy and current 2026 technologies: concrete steps for phones, WiFi, home devices, and proximity-based protection.

About Nicholas Merrill: Privacy Activist and Entrepreneur

Nicholas Merrill is a pioneering privacy advocate and system administrator best known for being the first person to challenge the National Security Letter (NSL) provision of the USA PATRIOT Act in 2004. After receiving a secret FBI demand for customer data from his ISP, Calyx Internet Access, Merrill spent over a decade fighting the accompanying lifelong gag order, eventually winning the right to speak publicly about the case in 2015.

Recent Developments: Calyx Institute Departure and Phreeli Launch

In 2025, Merrill made two significant moves:

  1. Left the Calyx Institute: After founding the nonprofit in 2010, Merrill departed in 2025 to pursue other projects, causing disruption to CalyxOS development. The organization faced leadership transitions, with both Merrill and lead developer Chirayu Desai leaving, resulting in paused updates and new signing key requirements. [1][2][3]
  2. Launched Phreeli.com: In December 2025, Merrill introduced Phreeli, the world's first privacy-by-design wireless carrier, featured in Wired. [4][5]

The service allows users to sign up with only a ZIP code, using a "Double-Blind Armadillo" cryptographic architecture to separate identity from network activity. [2][6][1]

Actionable Privacy Protection Steps

Based on Merrill's privacy-first philosophy and current 2026 technologies, here are concrete steps to protect yourself across phones, WiFi, and home devices, including proximity-based protection.

Phone Protection

1. Choose Privacy-Respecting Mobile Service

Use Phreeli for Cellular Service

  • What it is: Phreeli is a prepaid MVNO that requires only a ZIP code to sign up, accepts cryptocurrency payments, and uses zero-knowledge proofs to verify service without linking identity to usage. [6][1]
  • How it works: The "Double-Blind Armadillo" system routes operations through a blind mixing service, preventing linkage between user actions and identity. [7]
  • Action: Sign up at phreeli.com using minimal information and pay with cryptocurrency for maximum anonymity. [8][6]
  • Live URL: https://www.phreeli.com/privacy[9]

Alternative: Use Prepaid SIMs with Cash

  • If Phreeli isn't available in your area, purchase prepaid SIM cards with cash at retail locations, avoiding identity verification. [2]

2. Install Privacy-Focused Operating System

CalyxOS (Current Status: Use with Caution)

  • Current situation: As of January 2026, CalyxOS development is on hiatus due to leadership departures. Updates are paused and new signing keys are being implemented. [5][10]
  • Action: If already using CalyxOS, monitor calyxos.org/news for updates on key ceremony and Android 16 QPR1 ports. For new installations, consider alternatives like GrapheneOS until CalyxOS resumes stable releases. [10][11][5]
  • Live URL: https://calyxos.org/news/[10]

Key Features When Active:

  • De-Googled Android with microG
  • Default Signal integration for calls/texts
  • Tor and VPN support built-in
  • No Google Play Services tracking [12][13]

3. Minimize App Permissions and Metadata

Principle: Merrill's approach emphasizes that "if you do not share data, it cannot be compromised". [14]

Actionable Steps:

  • Audit app permissions: Disable location, contacts, microphone, and camera access for all non-essential apps
  • Use Signal for communications: Default to Signal for calls and texts to encrypt metadata [2]
  • Remove bloatware: Uninstall or disable pre-installed apps that collect telemetry
  • Use Shelter work profile: Isolate untrusted apps in a separate work profile to prevent data leakage

4. Enable Network-Level Encryption

Always-on VPN + Tor:

  • Configure CalyxOS or GrapheneOS to route all traffic through Tor via Orbot
  • Use Calyx Institute's no-logs VPN service (if still operational) or Mullvad VPN (accepts cash/crypto) [12]
  • Enable "Always-on VPN" and "Block connections without VPN" in network settings

WiFi Protection

1. Secure Home Router Configuration

Principle: Treat your router as a critical privacy boundary, following Merrill's ISP privacy advocacy. [3]

Actionable Steps:

  • Change default credentials: Replace factory admin username/password
  • Disable WPS: This insecure protocol can be brute-forced
  • Use WPA3 encryption: If available, otherwise WPA2-AES
  • Disable remote management: Prevent ISP or attackers from accessing router settings
  • Enable MAC address filtering: Only allow known devices (though MACs can be spoofed)
  • Disable SSID broadcast: Makes network less visible to casual scanners

2. DNS Privacy

Use Encrypted DNS:

  • Configure router to use DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) or DNS-over-TLS (DoT)
  • Recommended providers: Quad9 (privacy-focused) or Cloudflare (1.1.1.1)
  • This prevents ISP from seeing your DNS queries, a key metadata source [12]

3. Network Segmentation

Separate Networks for Different Device Types:

  • Main network: For trusted devices (laptops, phones with privacy OS)
  • IoT network: Separate SSID for smart home devices (see below)
  • Guest network: For visitors, isolated from main network
  • VLANs: If your router supports it, use VLANs to isolate networks at layer 2

4. Public WiFi Protection

Never Trust Public Networks:

  • Always use VPN: Connect to VPN before joining public WiFi
  • Disable auto-join: Prevent phone from automatically connecting to open networks
  • Use mobile hotspot instead: If possible, use your Phreeli connection rather than public WiFi
  • Verify network names: Confirm exact SSID with venue staff to avoid evil twin attacks

Home Device Protection

1. IoT Device Management

Principle: Apply Merrill's "privacy by design" to smart home devices. [12]

Actionable Steps:

  • Audit all connected devices: List every IoT device and its data collection practices
  • Disable cloud features: Use local control where possible (Home Assistant, openHAB)
  • Block internet access: Use router firewall rules to prevent IoT devices from phoning home
  • Change default passwords: Many IoT devices have hardcoded credentials
  • Update firmware: Check for updates monthly, or replace if unsupported
  • Physical mute switches: Use devices with hardware mute for microphones (e.g., smart speakers)

2. Smart TV and Streaming Devices

Disable Tracking:

  • Turn off ACR (Automatic Content Recognition) in TV settings
  • Disable personalized ads
  • Use Pi-hole or AdGuard Home to block telemetry domains at DNS level
  • Connect via Ethernet and disable WiFi to reduce broadcast visibility

3. Voice Assistants

Minimize Always-On Listening:

  • Use push-to-talk devices instead of wake-word activation
  • Review and delete voice recordings regularly
  • Disable voice purchasing and sensitive commands
  • Consider removing cloud-based assistants entirely and using local alternatives (Rhasspy, Mycroft)

Proximity-Based Protection

This addresses threats from being near others, even without using your device.

1. Location Tracking Prevention

Cellular Tower Tracking Mitigation:

  • Use Phreeli's architecture: Since Phreeli separates identity from SIM activity, tower logs can't easily be linked to you. [1][6]
  • Airplane mode when idle: Disable cellular radios when not expecting calls
  • Faraday bags: Use RF-blocking pouches (e.g., SLNT, Faraday Defense) when you need guaranteed isolation [2]
  • Disable location services: Turn off GPS, WiFi scanning, and Bluetooth scanning in settings

Bluetooth Beacon Awareness:

  • Disable Bluetooth when not in use: Prevents tracking via retail beacons and contact tracing
  • Disable "Find My" networks: Apple and Google crowdsource location via nearby devices
  • Use BLE spoofing: Some privacy OSes allow randomizing Bluetooth MAC addresses

2. WiFi Probe Request Privacy

Prevent Passive WiFi Tracking:

  • Disable WiFi when not connected: Stops probe requests that reveal your device's presence
  • Use MAC randomization: Enable "Randomized MAC" for each WiFi network (Android 10+)
  • Forget networks after use: Prevents automatic probing for known SSIDs

3. Audio Surveillance Protection

Protect Against Nearby Recording:

  • Use ultrasonic jammer: Devices like the "Ultrasonic Microphone Blocker" emit frequencies that interfere with microphones (legal in most jurisdictions)
  • White noise generators: Place near windows/doors to mask conversations from directional microphones
  • Physical barriers: Soundproofing materials, window film, and door sweeps reduce audio leakage
  • Signal-blocking paint: Paint walls with RF-blocking paint to create a Faraday room

4. Visual Privacy

Prevent Shoulder Surfing and Cameras:

  • Privacy screen protectors: Limit viewing angle to ~30 degrees
  • Camera covers: Physical shutters for laptop and phone cameras
  • IR-reflective glasses: Some glasses reflect infrared light used by hidden cameras
  • Laser detection: Use camera detectors to find hidden surveillance devices in Airbnbs/hotels

5. Social Engineering and Shoulder Surfing Defense

Protect Against Human Proximity Threats:

  • Use privacy screen filters: On phones and laptops in public spaces
  • Beware of "shoulder surfing": Position yourself with back to walls in public
  • Disable lock screen notifications: Prevents message previews from being read
  • Use duress passwords: Some OSes allow a secondary password that wipes data or opens a decoy profile

Advanced Protection: Zero-Trust Architecture

Based on Merrill's "privacy by design" philosophy, implement zero-trust principles: [12]

1. Assume Breach Mentality

  • Compartmentalize data: Use different devices for different purposes (work, personal, sensitive)
  • Encrypt everything: Full disk encryption on all devices, use VeraCrypt for external drives
  • Regular factory resets: Wipe and reinstall phone OS monthly to clear potential malware

2. Metadata Minimization

  • Use Matrix/Element instead of SMS: Decentralized, encrypted messaging with minimal metadata
  • Disable read receipts and typing indicators: Reduces behavioral metadata
  • Schedule message sending: Avoid pattern analysis by sending at random intervals
  • Use Tor Browser for all web activity: Never use regular browsers, even for "innocent" searches

3. Physical Security

  • Never leave devices unattended: Even for seconds in trusted environments
  • Use hardware security keys: YubiKey for 2FA instead of SMS codes
  • Secure boot: Enable on all devices that support it
  • Tamper-evident seals: Place stickers on device screws to detect physical access

Implementation Checklist for January 2026

Immediate Actions (This Week):

  1. Sign up for Phreeli at https://www.phreeli.com/privacy
  2. Audit all app permissions on your phone
  3. Enable MAC randomization for WiFi networks
  4. Change router admin password and disable remote management
  5. Install Signal and set as default SMS app

Short-term (This Month):

  1. Install GrapheneOS or await CalyxOS stable release
  2. Set up Pi-hole or AdGuard Home on home network
  3. Create separate VLANs for IoT devices
  4. Purchase Faraday bag for sensitive situations
  5. Disable Bluetooth and location services when not needed

Long-term (This Quarter):

  1. Migrate all communications to Matrix/Signal
  2. Replace cloud-dependent IoT devices with local-control alternatives
  3. Implement full-disk encryption on all devices
  4. Conduct quarterly privacy audits of all services and devices
  5. Attend FOSDEM 2026 (Jan 31) to learn about latest privacy developments

Key Live URLs (January 2026)

  • Phreeli Privacy Policy: https://www.phreeli.com/privacy/policy
  • Phreeli Main Site: https://www.phreeli.com/privacy
  • Calyx Institute About: https://calyxinstitute.org/about
  • CalyxOS News: https://calyxos.org/news/
  • Wired Feature on Phreeli: Wired story
  • Business Wire Announcement: Business Wire

Conclusion

Nicholas Merrill's evolution from challenging FBI surveillance to launching Phreeli demonstrates that effective privacy protection in 2026 requires systemic changes at the carrier and OS level, not just app-level tweaks. The core principle remains: minimize data collection at the source, separate identity from activity, and assume that any data collected will be compromised.

By implementing these steps—switching to Phreeli, using a privacy OS, securing your WiFi network, isolating IoT devices, and employing proximity-based defenses like Faraday bags—you create multiple layers of protection that align with Merrill's "privacy by design" philosophy. The goal is not paranoia, but digital autonomy: living your normal life without feeling watched or exploited. [14][1]

The organization faced leadership transitions, with both Merrill and lead developer Chirayu Desai leaving, resulting in paused updates and new signing key requirements. [1][2][3]
  2. Launched Phreeli.com: In December 2025, Merrill introduced Phreeli, the world's first privacy-by-design wireless carrier, featured in Wired. [4][5]

The service allows users to sign up with only a ZIP code, using a "Double-Blind Armadillo" cryptographic architecture to separate identity from network activity. [2][6][1]

Actionable Privacy Protection Steps

Based on Merrill's privacy-first philosophy and current 2026 technologies, here are concrete steps to protect yourself across phones, WiFi, and home devices, including proximity-based protection.

Phone Protection

1. Choose Privacy-Respecting Mobile Service

Use Phreeli for Cellular Service

  • What it is: Phreeli is a prepaid MVNO that requires only a ZIP code to sign up, accepts cryptocurrency payments, and uses zero-knowledge proofs to verify service without linking identity to usage. [6][1]
  • How it works: The "Double-Blind Armadillo" system routes operations through a blind mixing service, preventing linkage between user actions and identity. [7]
  • Action: Sign up at phreeli.com using minimal information and pay with cryptocurrency for maximum anonymity. [8][6]
  • Live URL: https://www.phreeli.com/privacy[9]

Alternative: Use Prepaid SIMs with Cash

  • If Phreeli isn't available in your area, purchase prepaid SIM cards with cash at retail locations, avoiding identity verification. [2]

2. Install Privacy-Focused Operating System

CalyxOS (Current Status: Use with Caution)

  • Current situation: As of January 2026, CalyxOS development is on hiatus due to leadership departures. Updates are paused and new signing keys are being implemented. [5][10]
  • Action: If already using CalyxOS, monitor calyxos.org/news for updates on key ceremony and Android 16 QPR1 ports. For new installations, consider alternatives like GrapheneOS until CalyxOS resumes stable releases. [10][11][5]
  • Live URL: https://calyxos.org/news/[10]

Key Features When Active:

  • De-Googled Android with microG
  • Default Signal integration for calls/texts
  • Tor and VPN support built-in
  • No Google Play Services tracking [12][13]

3. Minimize App Permissions and Metadata

Principle: Merrill's approach emphasizes that "if you do not share data, it cannot be compromised". [14]

Actionable Steps:

  • Audit app permissions: Disable location, contacts, microphone, and camera access for all non-essential apps
  • Use Signal for communications: Default to Signal for calls and texts to encrypt metadata [2]
  • Remove bloatware: Uninstall or disable pre-installed apps that collect telemetry
  • Use Shelter work profile: Isolate untrusted apps in a separate work profile to prevent data leakage

4. Enable Network-Level Encryption

Always-on VPN + Tor:

  • Configure CalyxOS or GrapheneOS to route all traffic through Tor via Orbot
  • Use Calyx Institute's no-logs VPN service (if still operational) or Mullvad VPN (accepts cash/crypto) [12]
  • Enable "Always-on VPN" and "Block connections without VPN" in network settings

WiFi Protection

1. Secure Home Router Configuration

Principle: Treat your router as a critical privacy boundary, following Merrill's ISP privacy advocacy. [3]

Actionable Steps:

  • Change default credentials: Replace factory admin username/password
  • Disable WPS: This insecure protocol can be brute-forced
  • Use WPA3 encryption: If available, otherwise WPA2-AES
  • Disable remote management: Prevent ISP or attackers from accessing router settings
  • Enable MAC address filtering: Only allow known devices (though MACs can be spoofed)
  • Disable SSID broadcast: Makes network less visible to casual scanners

2. DNS Privacy

Use Encrypted DNS:

  • Configure router to use DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) or DNS-over-TLS (DoT)
  • Recommended providers: Quad9 (privacy-focused) or Cloudflare (1.1.1.1)
  • This prevents ISP from seeing your DNS queries, a key metadata source [12]

3. Network Segmentation

Separate Networks for Different Device Types:

  • Main network: For trusted devices (laptops, phones with privacy OS)
  • IoT network: Separate SSID for smart home devices (see below)
  • Guest network: For visitors, isolated from main network
  • VLANs: If your router supports it, use VLANs to isolate networks at layer 2

4. Public WiFi Protection

Never Trust Public Networks:

  • Always use VPN: Connect to VPN before joining public WiFi
  • Disable auto-join: Prevent phone from automatically connecting to open networks
  • Use mobile hotspot instead: If possible, use your Phreeli connection rather than public WiFi
  • Verify network names: Confirm exact SSID with venue staff to avoid evil twin attacks

Home Device Protection

1. IoT Device Management

Principle: Apply Merrill's "privacy by design" to smart home devices. [12]

Actionable Steps:

  • Audit all connected devices: List every IoT device and its data collection practices
  • Disable cloud features: Use local control where possible (Home Assistant, openHAB)
  • Block internet access: Use router firewall rules to prevent IoT devices from phoning home
  • Change default passwords: Many IoT devices have hardcoded credentials
  • Update firmware: Check for updates monthly, or replace if unsupported
  • Physical mute switches: Use devices with hardware mute for microphones (e.g., smart speakers)

2. Smart TV and Streaming Devices

Disable Tracking:

  • Turn off ACR (Automatic Content Recognition) in TV settings
  • Disable personalized ads
  • Use Pi-hole or AdGuard Home to block telemetry domains at DNS level
  • Connect via Ethernet and disable WiFi to reduce broadcast visibility

3. Voice Assistants

Minimize Always-On Listening:

  • Use push-to-talk devices instead of wake-word activation
  • Review and delete voice recordings regularly
  • Disable voice purchasing and sensitive commands
  • Consider removing cloud-based assistants entirely and using local alternatives (Rhasspy, Mycroft)

Proximity-Based Protection

This addresses threats from being near others, even without using your device.

1. Location Tracking Prevention

Cellular Tower Tracking Mitigation:

  • Use Phreeli's architecture: Since Phreeli separates identity from SIM activity, tower logs can't easily be linked to you. [1][6]
  • Airplane mode when idle: Disable cellular radios when not expecting calls
  • Faraday bags: Use RF-blocking pouches (e.g., SLNT, Faraday Defense) when you need guaranteed isolation [2]
  • Disable location services: Turn off GPS, WiFi scanning, and Bluetooth scanning in settings

Bluetooth Beacon Awareness:

  • Disable Bluetooth when not in use: Prevents tracking via retail beacons and contact tracing
  • Disable "Find My" networks: Apple and Google crowdsource location via nearby devices
  • Use BLE spoofing: Some privacy OSes allow randomizing Bluetooth MAC addresses

2. WiFi Probe Request Privacy

Prevent Passive WiFi Tracking:

  • Disable WiFi when not connected: Stops probe requests that reveal your device's presence
  • Use MAC randomization: Enable "Randomized MAC" for each WiFi network (Android 10+)
  • Forget networks after use: Prevents automatic probing for known SSIDs

3. Audio Surveillance Protection

Protect Against Nearby Recording:

  • Use ultrasonic jammer: Devices like the "Ultrasonic Microphone Blocker" emit frequencies that interfere with microphones (legal in most jurisdictions)
  • White noise generators: Place near windows/doors to mask conversations from directional microphones
  • Physical barriers: Soundproofing materials, window film, and door sweeps reduce audio leakage
  • Signal-blocking paint: Paint walls with RF-blocking paint to create a Faraday room

4. Visual Privacy

Prevent Shoulder Surfing and Cameras:

  • Privacy screen protectors: Limit viewing angle to ~30 degrees
  • Camera covers: Physical shutters for laptop and phone cameras
  • IR-reflective glasses: Some glasses reflect infrared light used by hidden cameras
  • Laser detection: Use camera detectors to find hidden surveillance devices in Airbnbs/hotels

5. Social Engineering and Shoulder Surfing Defense

Protect Against Human Proximity Threats:

  • Use privacy screen filters: On phones and laptops in public spaces
  • Beware of "shoulder surfing": Position yourself with back to walls in public
  • Disable lock screen notifications: Prevents message previews from being read
  • Use duress passwords: Some OSes allow a secondary password that wipes data or opens a decoy profile

Advanced Protection: Zero-Trust Architecture

Based on Merrill's "privacy by design" philosophy, implement zero-trust principles: [12]

1. Assume Breach Mentality

  • Compartmentalize data: Use different devices for different purposes (work, personal, sensitive)
  • Encrypt everything: Full disk encryption on all devices, use VeraCrypt for external drives
  • Regular factory resets: Wipe and reinstall phone OS monthly to clear potential malware

2. Metadata Minimization

  • Use Matrix/Element instead of SMS: Decentralized, encrypted messaging with minimal metadata
  • Disable read receipts and typing indicators: Reduces behavioral metadata
  • Schedule message sending: Avoid pattern analysis by sending at random intervals
  • Use Tor Browser for all web activity: Never use regular browsers, even for "innocent" searches

3. Physical Security

  • Never leave devices unattended: Even for seconds in trusted environments
  • Use hardware security keys: YubiKey for 2FA instead of SMS codes
  • Secure boot: Enable on all devices that support it
  • Tamper-evident seals: Place stickers on device screws to detect physical access

Implementation Checklist for January 2026

Immediate Actions (This Week):

  1. Sign up for Phreeli at https://www.phreeli.com/privacy
  2. Audit all app permissions on your phone
  3. Enable MAC randomization for WiFi networks
  4. Change router admin password and disable remote management
  5. Install Signal and set as default SMS app

Short-term (This Month):

  1. Install GrapheneOS or await CalyxOS stable release
  2. Set up Pi-hole or AdGuard Home on home network
  3. Create separate VLANs for IoT devices
  4. Purchase Faraday bag for sensitive situations
  5. Disable Bluetooth and location services when not needed

Long-term (This Quarter):

  1. Migrate all communications to Matrix/Signal
  2. Replace cloud-dependent IoT devices with local-control alternatives
  3. Implement full-disk encryption on all devices
  4. Conduct quarterly privacy audits of all services and devices
  5. Attend FOSDEM 2026 (Jan 31) to learn about latest privacy developments

Key Live URLs (January 2026)

  • Phreeli Privacy Policy: https://www.phreeli.com/privacy/policy
  • Phreeli Main Site: https://www.phreeli.com/privacy
  • Calyx Institute About: https://calyxinstitute.org/about
  • CalyxOS News: https://calyxos.org/news/
  • Wired Feature on Phreeli: Wired story
  • Business Wire Announcement: Business Wire

Conclusion

Nicholas Merrill's evolution from challenging FBI surveillance to launching Phreeli demonstrates that effective privacy protection in 2026 requires systemic changes at the carrier and OS level, not just app-level tweaks. The core principle remains: minimize data collection at the source, separate identity from activity, and assume that any data collected will be compromised.

By implementing these steps—switching to Phreeli, using a privacy OS, securing your WiFi network, isolating IoT devices, and employing proximity-based defenses like Faraday bags—you create multiple layers of protection that align with Merrill's "privacy by design" philosophy. The goal is not paranoia, but digital autonomy: living your normal life without feeling watched or exploited. [14][1]

message.content: it seems that the nav and footer is different on many pages. shall we consider a different approach to have 1 nav and footer and avoid having to change every page individually each time we add a blog post or move pages around?

content.text:
Yes — a shared nav/footer component loaded from a single file would save a lot of pain. Since justNICE is a static site (no build step, no server-side includes), the cleanest options are: 1. **JavaScript includes** — A small JS file that injects the nav and footer HTML into placeholder `
`s on each page. Zero build tools, works with your current SFTP/GitHub deploy. 2. **Web Components** — Custom elements like `` and `` that encapsulate the HTML. More modern, same zero-build approach. 3. **Build step** (e.g., 11ty, simple shell script) — Templates with partials that compile to full HTML. More robust but adds a build dependency. Option 1 is the most pragmatic given your current setup — one `nav.js` and `footer.js` (or a single `components.js`), each page just has a `
` and `
` plus a script tag. Changes to nav/footer happen in one place. The tradeoff: a brief flash before JS loads the nav (FOUC), though you can mitigate that with a minimal inline CSS placeholder. Want me to go with the JS includes approach? Or do you prefer web components or a build step?

message.content: ideally, i'd like an approach that optimizes for speed loading on desktop and mobile.

content.text:
The fastest approach for a static site is **a build script that injects shared partials into each HTML file at deploy time**. The browser gets complete HTML with zero JS overhead — no fetch, no FOUC, no render-blocking scripts.

Here's the idea:
- `_partials/nav.html` and `_partials/footer.html` hold the shared markup
- Each page has `<!-- NAV -->` and `<!-- FOOTER -->` placeholder comments
- A simple Node.js (or shell) script replaces placeholders with partial contents before deploy
- GitHub Actions runs the build on push, deploys the output

This means:
- **Fastest possible load** — pure static HTML, nothing deferred
- **One place to edit** nav and footer
- **No client-side JS** for structural elements
- **Works with your existing GitHub deploy** — just add a build step

The only complexity is the one-time migration: strip nav/footer from all 73+ pages and replace with placeholder comments, then set up the build script and GitHub Action.

Want me to go ahead and build this?

148→ ICE Activity in Texas 149→

150→

151→ Texas has become the epicenter of federal immigration enforcement under the Trump administration’s second term. With SB 8 mandating sheriff participation in 287(g), daily ICE arrests jumping from 85 to 176, and mega-detention facilities under construction, the state is experiencing enforcement at a scale not seen in modern history. This hub tracks statewide developments and links to detailed regional pages. 152→
